We say a learning algorithm is consistent relative to distance measure d𝑑d if for all P∗∈ℳsuperscript𝑃ℳP^{*}\in{\cal M}, if data are distributed according to P∗superscript𝑃P^{*}, then the output Pnsubscript𝑃𝑛P_{n} converges to P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} in the sense that d​(P∗,Pn)→0→𝑑superscript𝑃subscript𝑃𝑛0d(P^{*},P_{n})\rightarrow 0 with P∗superscript𝑃P^{*}-probability 111. Thus, if P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} is the ‘true’ state of nature, then given enough data, the learning algorithm will learn a good approximation of P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} with very high probability.

Example 2.7

[Markov and Bernoulli models] Recall that a k𝑘k-th order Markov chain on 𝒳={0,1}𝒳01{\mathcal{X}}=\{0,1\} is a probabilistic source such that for every n>k𝑛𝑘n>k,

P(Xn=1∣Xn−1=xn−1,…,Xn−k=xn−k)=P(Xn=1∣Xn−1=xn−1,…,Xn−k=xn−k,…,X1=x1).P(X_{n}=1\mid X_{n-1}=x_{n-1},\ldots,X_{n-k}=x_{n-k})=\\ P(X_{n}=1\mid X_{n-1}=x_{n-1},\ldots,X_{n-k}=x_{n-k},\ldots,X_{1}=x_{1}). (2.7)

That is, the probability distribution on Xnsubscript𝑋𝑛X_{n} depends only on the k𝑘k symbols preceding n𝑛n. Thus, there are 2ksuperscript2𝑘2^{k} possible distributions of Xnsubscript𝑋𝑛X_{n}, and each such distribution is identified with a state of the Markov chain. To fully identify the chain, we also need to specify the starting state, defining the first k𝑘k outcomes X1,…,Xksubscript𝑋1…subscript𝑋𝑘X_{1},\ldots,X_{k}. The k𝑘k-th order Markov model is the set of all k𝑘k-th order Markov chains, i.e. all sources satisfying (2.7) equipped with a starting state.

The special case of the 00-th order Markov model is the Bernoulli or biased coin model, which we denote by ℬ(0)superscriptℬ0{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)} We can parameterize the Bernoulli model by a parameter θ∈[0,1]𝜃01\theta\in[0,1] representing the probability of observing a 111. Thus, ℬ(0)={P(⋅∣θ)∣θ∈[0,1]}{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)}=\{P(\cdot\mid\theta)\mid\theta\in[0,1]\}, with P​(xn∣θ)𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃P(x^{n}\mid\theta) by definition equal to

P​(xn∣θ)=∏i=1nP​(xi∣θ)=θn[1]​(1−θ)n[0],𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑖1𝑛𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑖𝜃superscript𝜃subscript𝑛delimited-[]1superscript1𝜃subscript𝑛delimited-[]0P(x^{n}\mid\theta)=\prod_{i=1}^{n}P(x_{i}\mid\theta)=\theta^{n_{[1]}}(1-\theta)^{n_{[0]}},

where n[1]subscript𝑛delimited-[]1n_{[1]} stands for the number of 111s, and n[0]subscript𝑛delimited-[]0n_{[0]} for the number of 00s in the sample. Note that the Bernoulli model is i.i.d. The log-likelihood is given by

log⁡P​(xn∣θ)=n[1]​log⁡θ+n[0]​log⁡(1−θ).𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃subscript𝑛delimited-[]1𝜃subscript𝑛delimited-[]01𝜃\log P(x^{n}\mid\theta)=n_{[1]}\log\theta+n_{[0]}\log(1-\theta). (2.8)

Taking the derivative of (2.8) with respect to θ𝜃\theta, we see that for fixed xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, the log-likelihood is maximized by setting the probability of 111 equal to the observed frequency. Since the logarithm is a monotonically increasing function, the likelihood is maximized at the same value: the ML estimator is given by θ^​(xn)=n[1]/n^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝑛delimited-[]1𝑛\hat{\theta}(x^{n})=n_{[1]}/n.

Similarly, the first-order Markov model ℬ(1)superscriptℬ1{\mathcal{B}}^{(1)} can be parameterized by a vector θ=(θ[1|0],θ[1|1])∈[0,1]2𝜃subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional10subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional11superscript012\theta=(\theta_{[1|0]},\theta_{[1|1]})\in[0,1]^{2} together with a starting state in {0,1}01\{0,1\}. Here θ[1|j]subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional1𝑗\theta_{[1|j]} represents the probability of observing a 111 following the symbol j𝑗j. The log-likelihood is given by

log⁡P​(xn∣θ)=n[1|1]​log⁡θ[1|1]+n[0|1]​log⁡(1−θ[1|1])+n[1|0]​log⁡θ[1|0]+n[0|0]​log⁡(1−θ[1|0]),𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional11subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional11subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional011subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional11subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional10subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional10subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional001subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional10\log P(x^{n}\mid\theta)=n_{[1|1]}\log\theta_{[1|1]}+n_{[0|1]}\log(1-\theta_{[1|1]})+n_{[1|0]}\log\theta_{[1|0]}+n_{[0|0]}\log(1-\theta_{[1|0]}),

n[i|j]subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional𝑖𝑗n_{[i|j]} denoting the number of times outcome i𝑖i is observed in state (previous outcome) j𝑗j. This is maximized by setting θ^=(θ^[1|0],θ^[1|1])^𝜃subscript^𝜃delimited-[]conditional10subscript^𝜃delimited-[]conditional11\hat{\theta}=(\hat{\theta}_{[1|0]},\hat{\theta}_{[1|1]}), with θ^[i|j]=n[i|j]=n[j​i]/n[j]subscript^𝜃delimited-[]conditional𝑖𝑗subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional𝑖𝑗subscript𝑛delimited-[]𝑗𝑖subscript𝑛delimited-[]𝑗\hat{\theta}_{[i|j]}=n_{[i|j]}=n_{[ji]}/n_{[j]} set to the conditional frequency of i𝑖i preceded by j𝑗j. In general, a k𝑘k-th order Markov chain has 2ksuperscript2𝑘2^{k} parameters and the corresponding likelihood is maximized by setting the parameter θ[i|j]subscript𝜃delimited-[]conditional𝑖𝑗\theta_{[i|j]} equal to the number of times i𝑖i was observed in state j𝑗j divided by the number of times the chain was in state j𝑗j.

Suppose now we are given data D=xn𝐷superscript𝑥𝑛D=x^{n} and we want to find the Markov chain that best explains D𝐷D. Since we do not want to restrict ourselves to chains of fixed order, we run a large risk of overfitting: simply picking, among all Markov chains of each order, the ML Markov chain that maximizes the probability of the data, we typically end up with a chain of order n−1𝑛1n-1 with starting state given by the sequence x1,…,xn−1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛1x_{1},\ldots,x_{n-1}, and P​(Xn=xn∣Xn−1=xn−1)=1𝑃subscript𝑋𝑛conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝑋𝑛1superscript𝑥𝑛11P(X_{n}=x_{n}\mid X^{n-1}=x^{n-1})=1. Such a chain will assign probability 1 to xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. Below we show that MDL makes a more reasonable choice.

2.4 Crude MDL

Based on the information-theoretic (Section 2.2) and statistical (Section 2.3) preliminaries discussed before, we now formalize a first, crude version of MDL.

Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be a class of probabilistic sources (not necessarily Markov chains). Suppose we observe a sample D=(x1,…,xn)∈𝒳n𝐷subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛D=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}. Recall ‘the crude8 two-part code MDL Principle’ from Section 1.3, page 1.3:  
  Crude9, Two-part Version of MDL Principle Let ℋ(1),ℋ(2),…superscriptℋ1superscriptℋ2…{\cal H}^{(1)},{\cal H}^{(2)},\ldots be a set of candidate models. The best point hypothesis H∈ℋ(1)∪ℋ(2)∪…𝐻superscriptℋ1superscriptℋ2…H\in{\cal H}^{(1)}\cup{\cal H}^{(2)}\cup\ldots to explain data D𝐷D is the one which minimizes the sum L​(H)+L​(D|H)𝐿𝐻𝐿conditional𝐷𝐻L(H)+L(D|H), where • L​(H)𝐿𝐻L(H) is the length, in bits, of the description of the hypothesis; and • L​(D|H)𝐿conditional𝐷𝐻L(D|H) is the length, in bits, of the description of the data when encoded with the help of the hypothesis. The best model to explain D𝐷D is the smallest model containing the selected H𝐻H.    
In this section, we implement this crude MDL Principle by giving a precise definition of the terms L​(H)𝐿𝐻L(H) and L​(D|H)𝐿conditional𝐷𝐻L(D|H). To make the first term precise, we must design a code C1subscript𝐶1C_{1} for encoding hypotheses H𝐻H such that L​(H)=LC1​(H)𝐿𝐻subscript𝐿subscript𝐶1𝐻L(H)=L_{C_{1}}(H). For the second term, we must design a set of codes C2,Hsubscript𝐶2𝐻C_{2,H} (one for each H∈ℳ𝐻ℳH\in{\cal M}) such that for all D∈𝒳n𝐷superscript𝒳𝑛D\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, L​(D|H)=LC2,H​(D)𝐿conditional𝐷𝐻subscript𝐿subscript𝐶2𝐻𝐷L(D|H)=L_{C_{2,H}}(D). We start by describing the codes C2,Hsubscript𝐶2𝐻C_{2,H}.

2.4.1 Description Length of Data given Hypothesis

Given a sample of size n𝑛n, each hypothesis H𝐻H may be viewed as a probability distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n}. We denote the corresponding probability mass function by P(⋅∣H)P(\cdot\mid H). We need to associate with P(⋅∣H)P(\cdot\mid H) a code, or really, just a codelength function for 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n}. We already know that there exists a code with length function L𝐿L such that for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, L​(xn)=−log⁡P​(xn∣H)𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝐻L(x^{n})=-\log P(x^{n}\mid H). This is the code that we will pick. It is a natural choice for two reasons:

  1. 1.

    With this choice, the code length L​(xn∣H)𝐿conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝐻L(x^{n}\mid H) is equal to minus the log-likelihood of xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} according to H𝐻H, which is a standard statistical notion of ‘goodness-of-fit’.

  2. 2.

    If the data turn out to be distributed according to P𝑃P, then the code L(⋅∣H)L(\cdot\mid H) will uniquely minimize the expected code length (Section 2.2).

  • The second item implies that our choice is, in a sense, the only reasonable choice10. To see this, suppose ℳℳ{\cal M} is a finite i.i.d. model containing, say, M𝑀M distributions. Suppose we assign an arbitrary but finite code length L​(H)𝐿𝐻L(H) to each H∈ℳ𝐻ℳH\in{\cal M}. Suppose X1,X2,…subscript𝑋1subscript𝑋2…X_{1},X_{2},\ldots are actually distributed i.i.d. according to some ‘true’ H∗∈ℳsuperscript𝐻ℳH^{*}\in{\cal M}. By the reasoning of Example 2.6, we see that MDL will select the true distribution P(⋅∣H∗)P(\cdot\mid H^{*}) for all large n𝑛n, with probability 1. This means that MDL is consistent for finite ℳℳ{\cal M}. If we were to assign codes to distributions in some other manner not satisfying L​(D∣H)=−log⁡P​(D∣H)𝐿conditional𝐷𝐻𝑃conditional𝐷𝐻L(D\mid H)=-\log P(D\mid H), then there would exist distributions P(⋅∣H)P(\cdot\mid H) such that L​(D|H)≠−log⁡P​(D|H)𝐿conditional𝐷𝐻𝑃conditional𝐷𝐻L(D|H)\neq-\log P(D|H). But by Figure 2.1, there must be some distribution P(⋅∣H′)P(\cdot\mid H^{\prime}) with L(⋅|H)=−logP(⋅∣H′)L(\cdot|H)=-\log P(\cdot\mid H^{\prime}). Now let ℳ={H,H′}ℳ𝐻superscript𝐻′{\cal M}=\{H,H^{\prime}\} and suppose data are distributed according to P(⋅∣H′)P(\cdot\mid H^{\prime}). Then, by the reasoning of Example 2.6, MDL would select H𝐻H rather than H′superscript𝐻′H^{\prime} for all large n𝑛n! Thus, MDL would be inconsistent even in this simplest of all imaginable cases – there would then be no hope for good performance in the considerably more complex situations we intend to use it for11.

2.4.2 Description Length of Hypothesis

In its weakest and crudest form, the two-part code MDL Principle does not give any guidelines as to how to encode hypotheses (probability distributions). Every code for encoding hypotheses is allowed, as long as such a code does not change with the sample size n𝑛n.

  • To see the danger in allowing codes to depend on n𝑛n, consider the Markov chain example: if we were allowed to use different codes for different n𝑛n, we could use, for each n𝑛n, a code assigning a uniform distribution to all Markov chains of order n−1𝑛1n-1 with all parameters equal to 00 or 111. Since there are only a finite number (2n−1)2^{n-1}) of these, this is possible. But then, for each n𝑛n, xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, MDL would select the ML Markov chain of order n−1𝑛1n-1. Thus, MDL would coincide with ML and, no matter how large n𝑛n, we would overfit.

Consistency of Two-part MDL

Remarkably, if we fix an arbitrary code for all hypotheses, identical for all sample sizes n𝑛n, this is sufficient to make MDL consistent12 for a wide variety of models, including the Markov chains. For example, let L𝐿L be the length function corresponding to some code for the Markov chains. Suppose some Markov chain P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} generates the data such that L​(P∗)<∞𝐿superscript𝑃L(P^{*})<\infty under our coding scheme. Then, broadly speaking, for every P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} of every order, with probability 1 there exists some n0subscript𝑛0n_{0} such that for all samples larger than n0subscript𝑛0n_{0}, two-part MDL will select P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} – here n0subscript𝑛0n_{0} may depend on P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} and L𝐿L.

While this results indicates that MDL may be doing something sensible, it certainly does not justify the use of arbitrary codes - different codes will lead to preferences of different hypotheses, and it is not at all clear how a code should be designed that leads to good inferences with small, practically relevant sample sizes.

?] have developed a precise theory of how to design codes C1subscript𝐶1C_{1} in a ‘clever’ way, anticipating the developments of ‘refined MDL’. Practitioners have often simply used ‘reasonable’ coding schemes, based on the following idea. Usually there exists some ‘natural’ decomposition of the models under consideration, ℳ=⋃k>0ℳ(k)ℳsubscript𝑘0superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}=\bigcup_{k>0}{\cal M}^{(k)} where the dimension of ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} grows with k𝑘k but is not necessarily equal to k𝑘k. In the Markov chain example, we have ℬ=⋃ℬ(k)ℬsuperscriptℬ𝑘\mathcal{B}=\bigcup{\mathcal{B}}^{(k)} where ℬ(k)superscriptℬ𝑘{\mathcal{B}}^{(k)} is the k𝑘k-th order, 2ksuperscript2𝑘2^{k}-parameter Markov model. Then within each submodel ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)}, we may use a fixed-length code for θ∈Θ(k)𝜃superscriptΘ𝑘\theta\in\Theta^{(k)}. Since the set Θ(k)superscriptΘ𝑘\Theta^{(k)} is typically a continuum, we somehow need to discretize it to achieve this.

Example 2.8

[a Very Crude Code for the Markov Chains] We can describe a Markov chain of order k𝑘k by first describing k𝑘k, and then describing a parameter vector θ∈[0,1]k′𝜃superscript01superscript𝑘′\theta\in[0,1]^{k^{\prime}} with k′=2ksuperscript𝑘′superscript2𝑘k^{\prime}=2^{k}. We describe k𝑘k using our simple code for the integers (Example 2.4). This takes 2​log⁡k+12𝑘12\log k+1 bits. We now have to describe the k′superscript𝑘′k^{\prime}-component parameter vector. We saw in Example 2.7 that for any xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, the best-fitting (ML) k𝑘k-th order Markov chain can be identified with k′superscript𝑘′k^{\prime} frequencies. It is not hard to see that these frequencies are uniquely determined by the counts n[1|0​…​00],n[1|0​…​01],…,n[1|1​…​11]subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional10…00subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional10…01…subscript𝑛delimited-[]conditional11…11n_{[1|0\ldots 00]},n_{[1|0\ldots 01]},\ldots,n_{[1|1\ldots 11]}. Each individual count must be in the (n+1)𝑛1(n+1)-element set {0,1,…,n}01…𝑛\{0,1,\ldots,n\}. Since we assume n𝑛n is given in advance13, we may use a simple fixed-length code to encode this count, taking log⁡(n+1)𝑛1\log(n+1) bits (Example 2.3). Thus, once k𝑘k is fixed, we can describe such a Markov chain by a uniform code using k′​log⁡(n+1)superscript𝑘′𝑛1k^{\prime}\log(n+1) bits. With the code just defined we get for any P∈ℬ𝑃ℬP\in\mathcal{B}, indexed by parameter Θ(k)superscriptΘ𝑘\Theta^{(k)},

L​(P)=L​(k,Θ(k))=2​log⁡k+1+k​log⁡(n+1),𝐿𝑃𝐿𝑘superscriptΘ𝑘2𝑘1𝑘𝑛1L(P)=L(k,\Theta^{(k)})=2\log k+1+k\log(n+1),

so that with these codes, MDL tells us to pick the k,θ(k)𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘k,\theta^{(k)} minimizing

L​(k,θ(k))+L​(D∣k,θ(k))=2​log⁡k+1+k​log⁡(n+1)−log⁡P​(D∣k,θ(k)),𝐿𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘𝐿conditional𝐷𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘2𝑘1𝑘𝑛1𝑃conditional𝐷𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘L(k,\theta^{(k)})+L(D\mid k,\theta^{(k)})=2\log k+1+k\log(n+1)-\log P(D\mid k,\theta^{(k)}), (2.9)

where the θ(k)superscript𝜃𝑘\theta^{(k)} that is chosen will be equal to the ML estimator for ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)}.

Why (not) this code?

We may ask two questions about this code. First, why did we only reserve codewords for θ𝜃\theta that are potentially ML estimators for the given data? The reason is that, given k′=2ksuperscript𝑘′superscript2𝑘k^{\prime}=2^{k}, the codelength L​(D∣k,θ(k))𝐿conditional𝐷𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘L(D\mid k,\theta^{(k)}) is minimized by θ^(k)​(D)superscript^𝜃𝑘𝐷\hat{\theta}^{(k)}(D), the ML estimator within θ(k)superscript𝜃𝑘\theta^{(k)}. Reserving codewords for θ∈[0,1]k′𝜃superscript01superscript𝑘′\theta\in[0,1]^{k^{\prime}} that cannot be ML estimates would only serve to lengthen L​(D∣k,θ(k))𝐿conditional𝐷𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘L(D\mid k,\theta^{(k)}) and can never shorten L​(k,θ(k))𝐿𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘L(k,\theta^{(k)}). Thus, the total description length needed to encode D𝐷D will increase. Since our stated goal is to minimize description lengths, this is undesirable.

However, by the same logic we may also ask whether we have not reserved too many codewords for θ∈[0,1]k′𝜃superscript01superscript𝑘′\theta\in[0,1]^{k^{\prime}}. And in fact, it turns out that we have: the distance between two adjacent ML estimators is O​(1/n)𝑂1𝑛O(1/n). Indeed, if we had used a coarser precision, only reserving codewords for parameters with distances O​(1/n)𝑂1𝑛O(1/\sqrt{n}), we would obtain smaller code lengths - (2.9) would become

L​(k,θ(k))+L​(D∣k,θ(k))=−log⁡P​(D∣k,θ^(k))+k2​log⁡n+ck,𝐿𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘𝐿conditional𝐷𝑘superscript𝜃𝑘𝑃conditional𝐷𝑘superscript^𝜃𝑘𝑘2𝑛subscript𝑐𝑘L(k,\theta^{(k)})+L(D\mid k,\theta^{(k)})=-\log P(D\mid k,\hat{\theta}^{(k)})+\frac{k}{2}\log n+c_{k}, (2.10)

where cksubscript𝑐𝑘c_{k} is a small constant depending on k𝑘k, but not n𝑛n [1991]. In Section 2.6 we show that (2.10) is in some sense ‘optimal’.

The Good News and the Bad News

The good news is (1) we have found a principled, non-arbitrary manner to encode data D𝐷D given a probability distribution H𝐻H, namely, to use the code with lengths −log⁡P​(D∣H)𝑃conditional𝐷𝐻-\log P(D\mid H); and (2), asymptotically, any code for hypotheses will lead to a consistent criterion. The bad news is that we have not found clear guidelines to design codes for hypotheses H∈ℳ𝐻ℳH\in{\cal M}. We found some intuitively reasonable codes for Markov chains, and we then reasoned that these could be somewhat ‘improved’, but what is conspicuously lacking is a sound theoretical principle for designing and improving codes.

We take the good news to mean that our idea may be worth pursing further. We take the bad news to mean that we do have to modify or extend the idea to get a meaningful, non-arbitrary and practically relevant model selection method. Such an extension was already suggested in Rissanen’s early works [1978, 1983] and refined by ?]. However, in these works, the principle was still restricted to two-part codes. To get a fully satisfactory solution, we need to move to ‘universal codes’, of which the two-part codes are merely a special case.

2.5 Information Theory II: Universal Codes and Models

We have just indicated why the two-part code formulation of MDL needs to be refined. It turns out that the key concept we need is that of universal coding. Broadly speaking, a code L¯¯𝐿\bar{L} that is universal relative to a set of candidate codes ℒℒ{\cal L} allows us to compress every sequence xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} almost as well as the code in ℒℒ{\cal L} that compresses that particular sequence most. Two-part codes are universal (Section 2.5.1), but there exist other universal codes such as the Bayesian mixture code (Section 2.5.2) and the Normalized Maximum Likelihood (NML) code (Section 2.5.3). We also discuss universal models, which are just the probability distributions corresponding to universal codes. In this section, we are not concerned with learning from data; we only care about compressing data as much as possible. We reconnect our findings with learning in Section 2.6.

Coding as Communication

Like many other topics in coding, ‘universal coding’ can best be explained if we think of descriptions as messages: we can always view a description as a message that some sender or encoder, say Mr. A, sends to some receiver or decoder, say Mr. B. Before sending any messages, Mr. A and Mr. B meet in person. They agree on the set of messages that A may send to B. Typically, this will be the set 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n} of sequences x1,…,xnsubscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}, where each xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i} is an outcome in the space 𝒳𝒳{\mathcal{X}} . They also agree upon a (prefix) code that will be used by A to send his messages to B. Once this has been done, A and B go back to their respective homes and A sends his messages to B in the form of binary strings. The unique decodability property of prefix codes implies that, when B receives a message, he should always be able to decode it in a unique manner.

Universal Coding

Suppose our encoder/sender is about to observe a sequence xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n} which he plans to compress as much as possible. Equivalently, he wants to send an encoded version of xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} to the receiver using as few bits as possible. Sender and receiver have a set of candidate codes ℒℒ\mathcal{L} for 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n} available14. They believe or hope that one of these codes will allow for substantial compression of xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. However, they must decide on a code for 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n} before sender observes the actual xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, and they do not know which code in ℒℒ\mathcal{L} will lead to good compression of the actual xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. What is the best thing they can do? They may be tempted to try the following: upon seeing xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, sender simply encodes/sends xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using the L∈ℒ𝐿ℒL\in\mathcal{L} that minimizes L​(xn)𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛L(x^{n}) among all L∈ℒ𝐿ℒL\in\mathcal{L}. But this naive scheme will not work: since decoder/receiver does not know what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} has been sent before decoding the message, he does not know which of the codes in ℒℒ\mathcal{L} has been used by sender/encoder. Therefore, decoder cannot decode the message: the resulting protocol does not constitute a uniquely decodable, let alone a prefix code. Indeed, as we show below, in general no code L¯¯𝐿\bar{L} exists such that for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, L¯​(xn)≤minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)¯𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{L}(x^{n})\leq\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n}): in words, there exists no code which, no matter what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} is, always mimics the best code for xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}.

Example 2.9

Suppose we think that our sequence can be reasonably well-compressed by a code corresponding to some biased coin model. For simplicity, we restrict ourselves to a finite number of such models. Thus, let ℒ={L1,…,L9}ℒsubscript𝐿1…subscript𝐿9\mathcal{L}=\{L_{1},\ldots,L_{9}\} where L1subscript𝐿1L_{1} is the code length function corresponding to the Bernoulli model P(⋅∣θ)P(\cdot\mid\theta) with parameter θ=0.1𝜃0.1\theta=0.1, L2subscript𝐿2L_{2} corresponds to θ=0.2𝜃0.2\theta=0.2 and so on. From (2.8) we see that, for example,

L8​(xn)=−log⁡P​(xn|0.8)=−n[0]​log⁡0.2−n[1]​log⁡0.8L9​(xn)=−log⁡P​(xn|0.9)=−n[0]​log⁡0.1−n[1]​log⁡0.9.subscript𝐿8superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛0.8subscript𝑛delimited-[]00.2subscript𝑛delimited-[]10.8subscript𝐿9superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛0.9subscript𝑛delimited-[]00.1subscript𝑛delimited-[]10.9\begin{array}[]{l}L_{8}(x^{n})=-\log P(x^{n}|0.8)=-n_{[0]}\log 0.2-n_{[1]}\log 0.8\\ L_{9}(x^{n})=-\log P(x^{n}|0.9)=-n_{[0]}\log 0.1-n_{[1]}\log 0.9.\end{array}

Both L8​(xn)subscript𝐿8superscript𝑥𝑛L_{8}(x^{n}) and L9​(xn)subscript𝐿9superscript𝑥𝑛L_{9}(x^{n}) are linearly increasing in the number of 111s in xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. However, if the frequency n1/nsubscript𝑛1𝑛n_{1}/n is approximately 0.80.80.8, then minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n}) will be achieved for L8subscript𝐿8L_{8}. If n1/n≈0.9subscript𝑛1𝑛0.9n_{1}/n\approx 0.9 then minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n}) is achieved for L9subscript𝐿9L_{9}. More generally, if n1/n≈j/10subscript𝑛1𝑛𝑗10n_{1}/n\approx j/10 then Ljsubscript𝐿𝑗L_{j} achieves the minimum15. We would like to send xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using a code L¯¯𝐿\bar{L} such that for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, we need at most L^​(xn)^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n}) bits, where L^​(xn)^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n}) is defined as L^​(xn):=minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)assign^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n}):=\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n}). Since −log-\log is monotonically decreasing, L^​(xn)=−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n})=-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n})). We already gave an informal explanation as to why a code with lengths L^^𝐿\hat{L} does not exist. We can now explain this more formally as follows: if such a code were to exist, it would correspond to some distribution P¯¯𝑃\bar{P}. Then we would have for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, L¯​(xn)=−log⁡P¯​(xn)¯𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{L}(x^{n})=-\log\bar{P}(x^{n}). But, by definition, for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n}, L¯​(xn)≤L^​(xn)=−log⁡P​(xn|θ^​(xn))¯𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{L}(x^{n})\leq\hat{L}(x^{n})=-\log P(x^{n}|\hat{\theta}(x^{n})) where θ^​(xn)∈{0.1,…,0.9}^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛0.1…0.9\hat{\theta}(x^{n})\in\{0.1,\ldots,0.9\}. Thus we get for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, −log⁡P¯​(xn)≤−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))​ or ​P¯​(xn)≥P​(xn∣θ^​(xn)),¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛 or ¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}(x^{n})\leq-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))\text{\ or \ }\bar{P}(x^{n})\geq P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n})), so that, since |ℒ|>1ℒ1|{\cal L}|>1,

∑xnP¯​(xn)≥∑xnP​(xn∣θ^​(xn))=∑xnmaxθ⁡P​(xn∣θ)>1,subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃1\sum_{x^{n}}\bar{P}(x^{n})\geq\sum_{x^{n}}P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))=\sum_{x^{n}}\max_{\theta}P(x^{n}\mid\theta)>1, (2.11)

where the last inequality follows because for any two θ1,θ2subscript𝜃1subscript𝜃2\theta_{1},\theta_{2} with θ1≠θ2subscript𝜃1subscript𝜃2\theta_{1}\neq\theta_{2}, there is at least one xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} with P​(xn∣θ1)>P​(xn∣θ2)𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃1𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃2P(x^{n}\mid\theta_{1})>P(x^{n}\mid\theta_{2}). (2.11) says that P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} is not a probability distribution. It follows that L¯¯𝐿\bar{L} cannot be a codelength function. The argument can be extended beyond the Bernoulli model of the example above: as long as |ℒ|>1ℒ1|{\cal L}|>1, and all codes in ℒℒ{\cal L} correspond to a non-defective distribution, (2.11) must still hold, so that there exists no code L¯¯𝐿\bar{L} with L¯​(xn)=L^​(xn)¯𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{L}(x^{n})=\hat{L}(x^{n}) for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. The underlying reason that no such code exists is the fact that probabilities must sum up to something ≤1absent1\leq 1; or equivalently, that there exists no coding scheme assigning short code words to many different messages – see Example 2.2.

Since there exists no code which, no matter what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} is, always mimics the best code for xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, it may make sense to look for the next best thing: does there exist a code which, for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, is ‘nearly’ (in some sense) as good as L^​(xn)^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n})? It turns out that in many cases, the answer is yes: there typically exists codes L¯¯𝐿\bar{L} such that no matter what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} arrives, L¯​(xn)¯𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{L}(x^{n}) is not much larger than L^​(xn)^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n}), which may be viewed as the code that is best ‘with hindsight’ (i.e., after seeing xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}). Intuitively, codes which satisfy this property are called universal codes - a more precise definition follows below. The first (but perhaps not foremost) example of a universal code is the two-part code that we have encountered in Section 2.4.

2.5.1 Two-part Codes as simple Universal Codes

Example 2.10

[finite ℒℒ\mathcal{L}] Let ℒℒ\mathcal{L} be as in Example 2.9. We can devise a code L¯2-psubscript¯𝐿2-p\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}} for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n} as follows: to encode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, we first encode the j∈{1,…,9}𝑗1…9j\in\{1,\ldots,9\} such that Lj​(xn)=minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)subscript𝐿𝑗superscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛L_{j}(x^{n})=\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n}), using a uniform code. This takes log⁡99\log 9 bits. We then encode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} itself using the code indexed by j𝑗j. This takes Ljsubscript𝐿𝑗L_{j} bits. Note that in contrast to the naive scheme discussed in Example 2.9, the resulting scheme properly defines a prefix code: a decoder can decode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} by first decoding j𝑗j, and then decoding xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using Ljsubscript𝐿𝑗L_{j}. Thus, for every possible xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, we obtain

L¯2-p​(xn)=minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)+log⁡9.subscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛9\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n})=\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n})+\log 9.

For all L∈ℒ𝐿ℒL\in\mathcal{L}, minxn⁡L​(xn)subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\min_{x^{n}}L(x^{n}) grows linearly in n𝑛n: minθ,xn−log⁡P​(xn∣θ)=−n​log⁡0.9≈0.15​nsubscript𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃𝑛0.90.15𝑛\min_{\theta,x^{n}}-\log P(x^{n}\mid\theta)=-n\log 0.9\approx 0.15n. Unless n𝑛n is very small, no matter what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} arises, the extra number of bits we need using L¯2-psubscript¯𝐿2-p\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}} compared to L^​(xn)^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n}) is negligible.

More generally, let ℒ={L1,…,LM}ℒsubscript𝐿1…subscript𝐿𝑀\mathcal{L}=\{L_{1},\ldots,L_{M}\} where M𝑀M can be arbitrarily large, and the Ljsubscript𝐿𝑗L_{j} can be any codelength functions we like; they do not necessarily represent Bernoulli distributions any more. By the reasoning of Example 2.10, there exists a (two-part) code such that for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n},

L¯2-p​(xn)=minL∈ℒ⁡L​(xn)+log⁡M.subscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛𝑀\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n})=\min_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n})+\log M. (2.12)

In most applications min⁡L​(xn)𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\min L(x^{n}) grows linearly in n𝑛n, and we see from (2.12) that, as soon as n𝑛n becomes substantially larger than log⁡M𝑀\log M, the relative difference in performance between our universal code and L^​(xn)^𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{L}(x^{n}) becomes negligible. In general, we do not always want to use a uniform code for the elements in ℒℒ{\cal L}; note that any arbitrary code on ℒℒ{\cal L} will give us an analogue of (2.12), but with a worst-case overhead larger than log⁡M𝑀\log M - corresponding to the largest codelength of any of the elements in ℒℒ{\cal L}.

Example 2.11

[Countably Infinite ℒℒ\mathcal{L}] We can also construct a 2-part code for arbitrary countably infinite sets of codes ℒ={L1,L2,…}ℒsubscript𝐿1subscript𝐿2…\mathcal{L}=\{L_{1},L_{2},\ldots\}: we first encode some k𝑘k using our simple code for the integers (Example 2.4). With this code we need 2​log⁡k+12𝑘12\log k+1 bits to encode integer k𝑘k. We then encode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using the code Lksubscript𝐿𝑘L_{k}. L¯2-psubscript¯𝐿2-p\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}} is now defined as the code we get if, for any xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, we encode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using the Lksubscript𝐿𝑘L_{k} minimizing the total two-part description length 2​log⁡k+1+Lk​(xn)2𝑘1subscript𝐿𝑘superscript𝑥𝑛2\log k+1+L_{k}(x^{n}).

In contrast to the case of finite ℒℒ\mathcal{L}, there does not exist a constant c𝑐c any more such that for all n,xn∈𝒳n𝑛superscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛n,x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}, L¯2-p​(xn)≤infL∈ℒL​(xn)+csubscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛subscriptinfimum𝐿ℒ𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛𝑐\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n})\leq\inf_{L\in\mathcal{L}}L(x^{n})+c. Instead we have the following weaker, but still remarkable property: for all k𝑘k, all n𝑛n, all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, L¯2-p​(xn)≤Lk​(xn)+2​log⁡k+1subscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝐿𝑘superscript𝑥𝑛2𝑘1\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n})\leq L_{k}(x^{n})+2\log k+1, so that also,

L¯2-p​(xn)≤infL∈{L1,…,Lk}L​(xn)+2​log⁡k+1.subscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛subscriptinfimum𝐿subscript𝐿1…subscript𝐿𝑘𝐿superscript𝑥𝑛2𝑘1\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n})\leq\inf_{L\in\{L_{1},\ldots,L_{k}\}}L(x^{n})+2\log k+1.

For any k𝑘k, as n𝑛n grows larger, the code L¯2-psubscript¯𝐿2-p\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}} starts to mimic whatever L∈{L1,…,Lk}𝐿subscript𝐿1…subscript𝐿𝑘L\in\{L_{1},\ldots,L_{k}\} compresses the data most. However, the larger k𝑘k, the larger n𝑛n has to be before this happens.

2.5.2 From Universal Codes to Universal Models

Instead of postulating a set of candidate codes ℒℒ\mathcal{L}, we may equivalently postulate a set ℳℳ{\cal M} of candidate probabilistic sources, such that ℒℒ\mathcal{L} is the set of codes corresponding to ℳℳ{\cal M}. We already implicitly did this in Example 2.9.

The reasoning is now as follows: we think that one of the P∈ℳ𝑃ℳP\in{\cal M} will assign a high likelihood to the data to be observed. Therefore we would like to design a code that, for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} we might observe, performs essentially as well as the code corresponding to the best-fitting, maximum likelihood (minimum codelength) P∈ℳ𝑃ℳP\in{\cal M} for xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. Similarly, we can think of universal codes such as the two-part code in terms of the (possibly defective, see Section 2.2 and Figure 2.1)) distributions corresponding to it. Such distributions corresponding to universal codes are called universal models. The use of mapping universal codes back to distributions is illustrated by the Bayesian universal model which we now introduce.

  • Universal model: Twice Misleading Terminology The words ‘universal’ and ‘model’ are somewhat of a misnomer: first, these codes/models are only ‘universal’ relative to a restricted ‘universe’ ℳℳ{\cal M}. Second, the use of the word ‘model’ will be very confusing to statisticians, who (as we also do in this paper) call a family of distributions such as ℳℳ{\cal M} a ’model’. But the phrase originates from information theory, where a ‘model’ often refers to a single distribution rather than a family. Thus, a ‘universal model’ is a single distribution, representing a statistical ‘model’ ℳℳ{\cal M}.

Example 2.12

[Bayesian Universal Model] Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be a finite or countable set of probabilistic sources, parameterized by some parameter set ΘΘ\Theta. Let W𝑊W be a distribution on ΘΘ\Theta. Adopting terminology from Bayesian statistics, W𝑊W is usually called a prior distribution. We can construct a new probabilistic source P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} by taking a weighted (according to W𝑊W) average or mixture over the distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M}. That is, we define for all n𝑛n, xn∈𝒳superscript𝑥𝑛𝒳x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}},

P¯Bayes​(xn):=∑θ∈ΘP​(xn∣θ)​W​(θ).assignsubscript¯𝑃Bayessuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃Θ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃𝑊𝜃\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n}):=\sum_{\theta\in\Theta}P(x^{n}\mid\theta)W(\theta). (2.13)

It is easy to check that P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is a probabilistic source according to our definition. In case ΘΘ\Theta is continuous, the sum gets replaced by an integral but otherwise nothing changes in the definition. In Bayesian statistics, P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is called the Bayesian marginal likelihood or Bayesian mixture [1994]. To see that P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is a universal model, note that for all θ0∈Θsubscript𝜃0Θ\theta_{0}\in\Theta,

−log⁡P¯Bayes​(xn):=−log​∑θ∈ΘP​(xn∣θ)​W​(θ)≤−log⁡P​(xn∣θ0)+cθ0assignsubscript¯𝑃Bayessuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃Θ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃𝑊𝜃𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃0subscript𝑐subscript𝜃0-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n}):=-\log\sum_{\theta\in\Theta}P(x^{n}\mid\theta)W(\theta)\leq-\log P(x^{n}\mid\theta_{0})+c_{\theta_{0}} (2.14)

where the inequality follows because a sum is at least as large as each of its terms, and cθ=−log⁡W​(θ)subscript𝑐𝜃𝑊𝜃c_{\theta}=-\log W(\theta) depends on θ𝜃\theta but not on n𝑛n. Thus, P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is a universal model or equivalently, the code with lengths −log⁡P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is a universal code. Note that the derivation in (2.14) only works if ΘΘ\Theta is finite or countable; the case of continuous ΘΘ\Theta is treated in Section 2.6.

Bayes is Better than Two-part

The Bayesian model is in a sense superior to the two-part code. Namely, in the two-part code we first encode an element of ℳℳ{\cal M} or its parameter set ΘΘ\Theta using some code L0subscript𝐿0L_{0}. Such a code must correspond to some ‘prior’ distribution W𝑊W on ℳℳ{\cal M} so that the two-part code gives codelengths

L¯2-p​(xn)=minθ∈Θ−log⁡P​(xn|θ)−log⁡W​(θ)subscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃Θ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃𝑊𝜃\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n})=\min_{\theta\in\Theta}-\log P(x^{n}|\theta)-\log W(\theta) (2.15)

where W𝑊W depends on the specific code L0subscript𝐿0L_{0} that was used. Using the Bayes code with prior W𝑊W, we get as in (2.14),

−log⁡P¯Bayes​(xn)=−log​∑θ∈ΘP​(xn∣θ)​W​(θ)≤minθ∈Θ−log⁡P​(xn|θ)−log⁡W​(θ).subscript¯𝑃Bayessuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝜃Θ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃𝑊𝜃subscript𝜃Θ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃𝑊𝜃-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n})=-\log\sum_{\theta\in\Theta}P(x^{n}\mid\theta)W(\theta)\leq\min_{\theta\in\Theta}-\log P(x^{n}|\theta)-\log W(\theta).

The inequality becomes strict whenever P​(xn|θ)>0𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃0P(x^{n}|\theta)>0 for more than one value of θ𝜃\theta. Comparing to (2.15), we see that in general the Bayesian code is preferable over the two-part code: for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} it never assigns codelengths larger than L¯2-p​(xn)subscript¯𝐿2-psuperscript𝑥𝑛\bar{L}_{\text{\rm 2-p}}(x^{n}), and in many cases it assigns strictly shorter codelengths for some xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. But this raises two important issues: (1) what exactly do we mean by ‘better’ anyway? (2) can we say that ‘some prior distributions are better than others’? These questions are answered below.

2.5.3 NML as an Optimal Universal Model

We can measure the performance of universal models relative to a set of candidate sources ℳℳ{\cal M} using the regret:

Definition 2.13

[Regret] Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be a class of probabilistic sources. Let P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} be a probability distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n} (P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} is not necessarily in ℳℳ{\cal M}). For given xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, the regret of P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} relative to ℳℳ{\cal M} is defined as

−log⁡P¯​(xn)−minP∈ℳ⁡{−log⁡P​(xn)}.¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝑃ℳ𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}(x^{n})-\min_{P\in{\cal M}}\{-\log P(x^{n})\}. (2.16)

The regret of P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} relative to ℳℳ{\cal M} for xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} is the additional number of bits needed to encode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using the code/distribution P¯¯𝑃\bar{P}, as compared to the number of bits that had been needed if we had used code/distribution in ℳℳ{\cal M} that was optimal (‘best-fitting’) with hind-sight. For simplicity, from now on we tacitly assume that for all the models ℳℳ{\cal M} we work with, there is a single θ^​(xn)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\theta}(x^{n}) maximizing the likelihood for every xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}. In that case (2.16) simplifies to

−log⁡P¯​(xn)−{−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))}.¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}(x^{n})-\{-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))\}.

We would like to measure the quality of a universal model P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} in terms of its regret. However, P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} may have small (even <0absent0<0) regret for some xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, and very large regret for other xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. We must somehow find a measure of quality that takes into account all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n}. We take a worst-case approach, and look for universal models P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} with small worst-case regret, where the worst-case is over all sequences. Formally, the maximum or worst-case regret of P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} relative to ℳℳ{\cal M} is defined as

ℛmax​(P¯):=maxxn∈𝒳n⁡{−log⁡P¯​(xn)−{−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))}}.assignsubscriptℛ¯𝑃subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\mathcal{R}_{\max}(\bar{P}){:=}\max_{x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}}\bigl{\{}-\log\bar{P}(x^{n})-\{-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))\}\bigr{\}}.

If we use ℛmaxsubscriptℛ\mathcal{R}_{\max} as our quality measure, then the ‘optimal’ universal model relative to ℳℳ{\cal M}, for given sample size n𝑛n, is the distribution minimizing

minP¯⁡ℛmax​(P¯)=minP¯⁡maxxn∈𝒳n⁡{−log⁡P¯​(xn)−{−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))}}subscript¯𝑃subscriptℛ¯𝑃subscript¯𝑃subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\min_{\bar{P}}\ \mathcal{R}_{\max}(\bar{P})=\min_{\bar{P}}\ \max_{x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}}\ \bigl{\{}-\log\bar{P}(x^{n})-\{-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))\}\bigr{\}} (2.17)

where the minimum is over all defective distributions on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\mathcal{X}}^{n}. The P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} minimizing (2.17) corresponds to the code minimizing the additional number of bits compared to code in ℳℳ{\cal M} that is best in hindsight in the worst-case over all possible xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. It turns out that we can solve for P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} in (2.17). To this end, we first define the complexity of a given model ℳℳ{\cal M} as

COMPn​(ℳ):=log​∑xn∈𝒳nP​(xn∣θ^​(xn)).assignsubscriptCOMP𝑛ℳsubscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}){:=}\log{\sum_{x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}}P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))}. (2.18)

This quantity plays a fundamental role in refined MDL, Section 2.6. To get a first idea of why COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} is called model complexity, note that the more sequences xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} with large P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n})), the larger COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}). In other words, the more sequences that can be fit well by an element of ℳℳ{\cal M}, the larger ℳℳ{\cal M}’s complexity.

Proposition 2.14

[1987] Suppose that COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) is finite. Then the minimax regret (2.17) is uniquely achieved for the distribution P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} given by

P¯nml​(xn):=P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))∑yn∈𝒳nP​(yn∣θ^​(yn)).assignsubscript¯𝑃nmlsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑦𝑛\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}){:=}\frac{P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))}{\sum_{y^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}}P(y^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(y^{n}))}. (2.19)

The distribution P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} is known as the Shtarkov distribution or the normalized maximum likelihood (NML) distribution.

Proof

Plug in P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} in (2.17) and notice that for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n},

−log⁡P¯​(xn)−{−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))}=ℛmax​(P¯)=COMPn​(ℳ),¯𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛subscriptℛ¯𝑃subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ-\log\bar{P}(x^{n})-\{-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))\}=\mathcal{R}_{\max}(\bar{P})=\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}), (2.20)

so that P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} achieves the same regret, equal to COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}), no matter what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} actually obtains. Since every distribution P𝑃P on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n} with P≠P¯nml𝑃subscript¯𝑃nmlP\neq\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} must satisfy P​(zn)<P¯nml​(zn)𝑃superscript𝑧𝑛subscript¯𝑃nmlsuperscript𝑧𝑛P(z^{n})<\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(z^{n}) for at least one zn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑧𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛z^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n}, it follows that

ℛmax​(P)≥−log⁡P​(zn)+log⁡P​(zn∣θ^​(zn))>−log⁡P¯nml​(zn)+log⁡P​(zn∣θ^​(zn))=ℛmax​(P¯nml).subscriptℛ𝑃𝑃superscript𝑧𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑧𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑧𝑛absentsubscript¯𝑃nmlsuperscript𝑧𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑧𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑧𝑛subscriptℛsubscript¯𝑃nml\mathcal{R}_{\max}(P)\geq-\log P(z^{n})+\log P(z^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(z^{n}))>\\ -\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(z^{n})+\log P(z^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(z^{n}))=\mathcal{R}_{\max}(\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}). (2.21)

□□\Box

P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} is quite literally a ‘normalized maximum likelihood’ distribution: it tries to assign to each xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} the probability of xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} according to the ML distribution for xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. By (2.11), this is not possible: the resulting ‘probabilities’ add to something larger than 111. But we can normalize these ‘probabilities’ by dividing by their sum ∑yn∈𝒳nP​(yn∣θ^​(yn))subscriptsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑦𝑛\sum_{y^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}}P(y^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(y^{n})), and then we obtain a probability distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n} after all.

Whenever 𝒳𝒳{\cal X} is finite, the sum COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) is finite so that the NML distribution is well-defined. If 𝒳𝒳{\cal X} is countably infinite or continuous-valued, the sum COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) may be infinite and then the NML distribution may be undefined. In that case, there exists no universal model achieving constant regret as in (2.20). If ℳℳ{\cal M} is parametric, then P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} is typically well-defined as long as we suitably restrict the parameter space. The parametric case forms the basis of ‘refined MDL’ and will be discussed at length in the next section.

2.6 Simple Refined MDL and its Four Interpretations

In Section 2.4, we indicated that ‘crude’ MDL needs to be refined. In Section 2.5 we introduced universal models. We now show how universal models, in particular the minimax optimal universal model P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, can be used to define a refined version of MDL model selection. Here we only discuss the simplest case: suppose we are given data D=(x1,…,xn)𝐷subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛D=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}) and two models ℳ(1)superscriptℳ1{\cal M}^{(1)} and ℳ(2)superscriptℳ2{\cal M}^{(2)} such that COMPn​(ℳ(1))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ1\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(1)}) and COMPn​(ℳ(2))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ2\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(2)}) (Equation 2.18) are both finite. For example, we could have some binary data and ℳ(1)superscriptℳ1{\cal M}^{(1)} and ℳ(2)superscriptℳ2{\cal M}^{(2)} are the first- and second-order Markov models (Example 2.7), both considered possible explanations for the data. We show how to deal with an infinite number of models and/or models with infinite COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} in Section 2.7.

Denote by P¯nml(⋅∣ℳ(j))\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}) the NML distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n} corresponding to model ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)}. Refined MDL tells us to pick the model ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} maximizing the normalized maximum likelihood P¯nml​(D∣ℳ(j))subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷superscriptℳ𝑗\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}), or, by (2.19), equivalently, minimizing

−log⁡P¯nml​(D∣ℳ(j))=−log⁡P​(D∣θ^(j)​(D))+COMPn​(ℳ(j))subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷superscriptℳ𝑗𝑃conditional𝐷superscript^𝜃𝑗𝐷subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}^{(j)})=-\log P(D\mid\hat{\theta}^{(j)}(D))+\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(j)}) (2.22)

From a coding theoretic point of view, we associate with each ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} a code with lengths P¯nml(⋅∣ℳ(j))\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}), and we pick the model minimizing the codelength of the data. The codelength −log⁡P¯nml​(D∣ℳ(j))subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷superscriptℳ𝑗-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}) has been called the stochastic complexity of the data D𝐷D relative to model ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} [1987], whereas COMPn​(ℳ(j))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(j)}) is called the parametric complexity or model cost of ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} (in this survey we simply call it ‘complexity’). We have already indicated in the previous section that COMPn​(ℳ(j))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(j)}) measures something like the ‘complexity’ of model ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)}. On the other hand, −log⁡P​(D∣θ^(j)​(D))𝑃conditional𝐷superscript^𝜃𝑗𝐷-\log P(D\mid\hat{\theta}^{(j)}(D)) is minus the maximized log-likelihood of the data, so it measures something like (minus) fit or error – in the linear regression case, it can be directly related to the mean squared error, Section 2.8. Thus, (2.22) embodies a trade-off between lack of fit (measured by minus log-likelihood) and complexity (measured by COMPn​(ℳ(j))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(j)})). The confidence in the decision is given by the codelength difference

|−log⁡P¯nml​(D∣ℳ(1))−[−log⁡P¯nml​(D∣ℳ(2))]|.subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷superscriptℳ1delimited-[]subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷superscriptℳ2\biggl{|}-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}^{(1)})-[-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}^{(2)})]\biggr{|}.

In general, −log⁡P¯nml​(D∣ℳ)subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷ℳ-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}) can only be evaluated numerically – the only exception this author is aware of is when ℳℳ{\cal M} is the Gaussian family, Example 2.20. In many cases even numerical evaluation is computationally problematic. But the re-interpretations of P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} we provide below also indicate that in many cases, −log⁡P¯​(D∣ℳ)¯𝑃conditional𝐷ℳ-\log\bar{P}(D\mid{\cal M}) is relatively easy to approximate.

Example 2.15

[Refined MDL and GLRT] Generalized likelihood ratio testing [1990] tells us to pick the ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} maximizing log⁡P​(D∣θ^(j)​(D))+c𝑃conditional𝐷superscript^𝜃𝑗𝐷𝑐\log P(D\mid\hat{\theta}^{(j)}(D))+c where c𝑐c is determined by the desired type-I and type-II errors. In practice one often applies a naive variation17, simply picking the model ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} maximizing log⁡P​(D∣θ^(j)​(D))𝑃conditional𝐷superscript^𝜃𝑗𝐷\log P(D\mid\hat{\theta}^{(j)}(D)). This amounts to ignoring the complexity terms COMPn​(ℳ(j))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(j)}) in (2.22): MDL tries to avoid overfitting by picking the model maximizing the normalized rather than the ordinary likelihood. The more distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M} that fit the data well, the larger the normalization term.

The hope is that the normalization term COMPn​(ℳ(j))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(j)}) strikes the right balance between complexity and fit. Whether it really does depends on whether COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} is a ‘good’ measure of complexity. In the remainder of this section we shall argue that it is, by giving four different interpretations of COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} and of the resulting trade-off (2.22):

  1. 1.

    Compression interpretation.

  2. 2.

    Counting interpretation.

  3. 3.

    Bayesian interpretation.

  4. 4.

    Prequential (predictive) interpretation.

2.6.1 Compression Interpretation

Rissanen’s original goal was to select the model that detects the most regularity in the data; he identified this with the ‘model that allows for the most compression of data xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}’. To make this precise, a code is associated with each model. The NML code with lengths −logP¯nml(⋅∣ℳ(j))-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}) seems to be a very reasonable choice for such a code because of the following two properties:

  1. 1.

    The better the best-fitting distribution in ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} fits the data, the shorter the codelength −log⁡P¯nml​(D∣ℳ(j))subscript¯𝑃nmlconditional𝐷superscriptℳ𝑗-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(D\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}).

  2. 2.

    No distribution in ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} is given a prior preference over any other distribution, since the regret of P¯nml(⋅∣ℳ(j))\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}) is the same for all D∈𝒳n𝐷superscript𝒳𝑛D\in{\cal X}^{n} (Equation (2.20)). P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} is the only complete prefix code with this property, which may be restated as: P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} treats all distributions within each ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} on the same footing!

Therefore, if one is willing to accept the basic ideas underlying MDL as first principles, then the use of NML in model selection is now justified to some extent. Below we give additional justifications that are not directly based on data compression; but we first provide some further interpretation of −log⁡P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}.

Compression and Separating Structure from Noise

We present the following ideas in an imprecise fashion – ?] recently showed how to make them precise. The stochastic complexity of data D𝐷D relative to ℳℳ{\cal M}, given by (2.22) can be interpreted as the amount of information in the data relative to ℳℳ{\cal M}, measured in bits. Although a one-part codelength, it still consists of two terms: a term COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) measuring the amount of structure or meaningful information in the data (as ‘seen through ℳℳ{\cal M}’), and a term −log⁡P​(D∣θ^​(D))𝑃conditional𝐷^𝜃𝐷-\log P(D\mid\hat{\theta}(D)) measuring the amount of noise or accidental information in the data. To see that this second term measures noise, consider the regression example, Example 1.2, again. As will be seen in Section 2.8, Equation (2.43), in that case −log⁡P​(D∣θ^​(D))𝑃conditional𝐷^𝜃𝐷-\log P(D\mid\hat{\theta}(D)) becomes equal to a linear function of the mean squared error of the best-fitting polynomial in the set of k𝑘k-th degree polynomials. To see that the first term measures structure, we reinterpret it below as the number of bits needed to specify a ‘distinguishable’ distribution in ℳℳ{\cal M}, using a uniform code on all ‘distinguishable’ distributions.

2.6.2 Counting Interpretation

The parametric complexity can be interpreted as measuring (the log of) the number of distinguishable distributions in the model. Intuitively, the more distributions a model contains, the more patterns it can fit well so the larger the risk of overfitting. However, if two distributions are very ‘close’ in the sense that they assign high likelihood to the same patterns, they do not contribute so much to the complexity of the overall model. It seems that we should measure complexity of a model in terms of the number of distributions it contains that are ‘essentially different’ (distinguishable), and we now show that COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} measures something like this. Consider a finite model ℳℳ{\cal M} with parameter set Θ={θ1,…,θM}Θsubscript𝜃1…subscript𝜃𝑀\Theta=\{\theta_{1},\ldots,\theta_{M}\}. Note that

∑xn∈𝒳nP​(xn|θ^​(xn))=∑j=1..M∑xn:θ^​(xn)=θjP​(xn|θj)=∑j=1..M(1−∑xn:θ^​(xn)≠θjP​(xn|θj))=M−∑jP​(θ^​(xn)≠θj|θj).\sum_{x^{n}\in{\mathcal{X}}^{n}}P(x^{n}|\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))=\sum_{j=1..M}\sum_{x^{n}:\hat{\theta}(x^{n})=\theta_{j}}P(x^{n}|\theta_{j})=\\ \sum_{j=1..M}\bigl{(}1-\sum_{x^{n}:\hat{\theta}(x^{n})\neq\theta_{j}}P(x^{n}|\theta_{j})\bigr{)}=M-\sum_{j}P(\hat{\theta}(x^{n})\neq\theta_{j}|\theta_{j}). (2.23)

We may think of P​(θ^​(xn)≠θj|θj)𝑃^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛conditionalsubscript𝜃𝑗subscript𝜃𝑗P(\hat{\theta}(x^{n})\neq\theta_{j}|\theta_{j}) as the probability, according to θjsubscript𝜃𝑗\theta_{j}, that the data look as if they come from some θ≠θj𝜃subscript𝜃𝑗\theta\neq\theta_{j}. Thus, it is the probability that θjsubscript𝜃𝑗\theta_{j} is mistaken for another distribution in ΘΘ\Theta. Therefore, for finite ℳℳ{\cal M}, Rissanen’s model complexity is the logarithm of the number of distributions minus the summed probability that some θjsubscript𝜃𝑗\theta_{j} is ‘mistaken’ for some θ≠θj𝜃subscript𝜃𝑗\theta\neq\theta_{j}. Now suppose ℳℳ{\cal M} is i.i.d. By the law of large numbers [1968], we immediately see that the ‘sum of mistake probabilities’ ∑jP​(θ^​(xn)≠θj|θj)subscript𝑗𝑃^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛conditionalsubscript𝜃𝑗subscript𝜃𝑗\sum_{j}P(\hat{\theta}(x^{n})\neq\theta_{j}|\theta_{j}) tends to 0 as n𝑛n grows. It follows that for large n𝑛n, the model complexity converges to log⁡M𝑀\log M. For large n𝑛n, the distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M} are ‘perfectly distinguishable’ (the probability that a sample coming from one is more representative of another is negligible), and then the parametric complexity COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) of ℳℳ{\cal M} is simply the log\log of the number of distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M}.

Example 2.16

[NML vs. Two-part Codes] Incidentally, this shows that for finite i.i.d. ℳℳ{\cal M}, the two-part code with uniform prior W𝑊W on ℳℳ{\cal M} is asymptotically minimax optimal: for all n𝑛n, the regret of the 2-part code is log⁡M𝑀\log M (Equation 2.12), whereas we just showed that for n→∞→𝑛n\rightarrow\infty, ℛ​(P¯nml)=COMPn​(ℳ)→log⁡Mℛsubscript¯𝑃nmlsubscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ→𝑀\mathcal{R}(\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}})=\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M})\rightarrow\log M. However, for small n𝑛n, some distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M} may be mistaken for one another; the number of distinguishable distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M} is then smaller than the actual number of distributions, and this is reflected in COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) being (sometimes much) smaller than log⁡M𝑀\log M.

For the more interesting case of parametric models, containing infinitely many distributions, Balasubramanian [?, ?] has a somewhat different counting interpretation of COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) as a ratio between two volumes. ?] give a more direct counting interpretation of COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}). These extensions are both based on the asymptotic expansion of P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, which we now discuss.

Asymptotic Expansion of P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} and COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n}

Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be a k𝑘k-dimensional parametric model. Under regularity conditions on ℳℳ{\cal M} and the parameterization Θ→ℳ→Θℳ\Theta\rightarrow{\cal M}, to be detailed below, we obtain the following asymptotic expansion of COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} [1996, 1997, 1998, 2000]:

COMPn​(ℳ)=k2​log⁡n2​π+log​∫θ∈Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θ+o​(1).subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ𝑘2𝑛2𝜋subscript𝜃Θ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃𝑜1\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M})=\frac{k}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}+\log\int_{\theta\in\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta+o(1). (2.24)

Here k𝑘k is the number of parameters (degrees of freedom) in model ℳℳ{\cal M}, n𝑛n is the sample size, and o​(1)→0→𝑜10o(1)\rightarrow 0 as n→∞→𝑛n\rightarrow\infty. |I​(θ)|𝐼𝜃|I(\theta)| is the determinant of the k×k𝑘𝑘k\times k Fisher information matrix18 I𝐼I evaluated at θ𝜃\theta. In case ℳℳ{\cal M} is an i.i.d. model, I𝐼I is given by

Ii​j​(θ∗):=Eθ∗​{−∂2∂θi​∂θj​log⁡P​(X|θ)}θ=θ∗.assignsubscript𝐼𝑖𝑗superscript𝜃subscriptEsuperscript𝜃subscriptsuperscript2subscript𝜃𝑖subscript𝜃𝑗𝑃conditional𝑋𝜃𝜃superscript𝜃I_{ij}(\theta^{*}){:=}\text{\rm E}_{\theta^{*}}\biggl{\{}-\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial\theta_{i}\partial\theta_{j}}\log P(X|\theta)\biggr{\}}_{\theta=\theta^{*}}.

This is generalized to non-i.i.d. models as follows:

Ii​j​(θ∗):=limn→∞1n​Eθ∗​{−∂2∂θi​∂θj​log⁡P​(Xn|θ)}θ=θ∗.assignsubscript𝐼𝑖𝑗superscript𝜃subscript→𝑛1𝑛subscriptEsuperscript𝜃subscriptsuperscript2subscript𝜃𝑖subscript𝜃𝑗𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑋𝑛𝜃𝜃superscript𝜃I_{ij}(\theta^{*}){:=}\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\frac{1}{n}\text{\rm E}_{\theta^{*}}\biggl{\{}-\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial\theta_{i}\partial\theta_{j}}\log P(X^{n}|\theta)\biggr{\}}_{\theta=\theta^{*}}.

(2.24) only holds if the model ℳℳ{\cal M}, its parameterization ΘΘ\Theta and the sequence x1,x2,…subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2…x_{1},x_{2},\ldots all satisfy certain conditions. Specifically, we require:

  1. 1.

    COMPn​(ℳ)<∞subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M})<\infty and ∫|I​(θ)|​𝑑θ<∞𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃\int\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta<\infty;

  2. 2.

    θ^​(xn)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\theta}(x^{n}) does not come arbitrarily close to the boundary of ΘΘ\Theta: for some ϵ>0italic-ϵ0\epsilon>0, for all large n𝑛n, θ^​(xn)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\theta}(x^{n}) remains farther than ϵitalic-ϵ\epsilon from the boundary of ΘΘ\Theta.

  3. 3.

    ℳℳ{\cal M} and ΘΘ\Theta satisfy certain further conditions. A simple sufficient condition is that ℳℳ{\cal M} be an exponential family [1990]. Roughly, this is a family that can be parameterized so that for all x𝑥x, P​(x∣β)=exp⁡(β​t​(x))​f​(x)​g​(β)𝑃conditional𝑥𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑥𝑓𝑥𝑔𝛽P(x\mid\beta)=\exp(\beta t(x))f(x)g(\beta), where t:𝒳→ℝ:𝑡→𝒳ℝt:{\cal X}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is a function of X𝑋X. The Bernoulli model is an exponential family, as can be seen by setting β:=ln⁡(1−θ)−ln⁡θassign𝛽1𝜃𝜃\beta:=\ln(1-\theta)-\ln\theta and t​(x)=x𝑡𝑥𝑥t(x)=x. Also the multinomial, Gaussian, Poisson, Gamma, exponential, Zipf and many other models are exponential families; but, for example, mixture models are not.

More general conditions are given by [1997] [?, ?, ?]. Essentially, if ℳℳ{\cal M} behaves ‘asymptotically’ like an exponential family, then (2.24) still holds. For example, (2.24) holds for the Markov models and for AR and ARMA processes.

Example 2.17

[Complexity of the Bernoulli Model] The Bernoulli model ℬ(0)superscriptℬ0{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)} can be parameterized in a 1-1 way by the unit interval (Example 2.7). Thus, k=1𝑘1k=1. An easy calculation shows that the Fisher information is given by θ​(1−θ)𝜃1𝜃\theta(1-\theta). Plugging this into (2.24) and calculating ∫|θ​(1−θ)|​𝑑θ𝜃1𝜃differential-d𝜃\int\sqrt{|\theta(1-\theta)|}d\theta gives

COMPn​(ℬ(0))=12​log⁡n+12​log⁡π2−3+o​(1)=12​log⁡n−2.674251935+o​(1).subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℬ012𝑛12𝜋23𝑜112𝑛2.674251935𝑜1\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\mathcal{B}}^{(0)})=\frac{1}{2}\log n+\frac{1}{2}\log\frac{\pi}{2}-3+o(1)=\frac{1}{2}\log n-2.674251935+o(1).

Computing the integral of the Fisher determinant is not easy in general. ?] compute it for several practically relevant models.

Whereas for finite ℳℳ{\cal M}, COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) remains finite, for parametric models it generally grows logarithmically in n𝑛n. Since typically −log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n})) grows linearly in n𝑛n, it is still the case that for fixed dimensionality k𝑘k (i.e. for a fixed ℳℳ{\cal M} that is k𝑘k-dimensional) and large n𝑛n, the part of the codelength −log⁡P¯nml​(xn∣ℳ)subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳ-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}) due to the complexity of ℳℳ{\cal M} is very small compared to the part needed to encode data xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} with θ^​(xn)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\theta}(x^{n}). The term ∫Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θsubscriptΘ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃\int_{\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta may be interpreted as the contribution of the functional form of ℳℳ{\cal M} to the model complexity [2004]. It does not grow with n𝑛n so that, when selecting between two models, it becomes irrelevant and can be ignored for very large n𝑛n. But for small n𝑛n, it can be important, as can be seen from Example 1.4, Fechner’s and Stevens’ model. Both models have two parameters, yet the ∫Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θsubscriptΘ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃\int_{\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta-term is much larger for Fechner’s than for Stevens’ model. In the experiments of ?], the parameter set was restricted to 0<a<∞,0<b<3formulae-sequence0𝑎0𝑏30<a<\infty,0<b<3 for Stevens’ model and 0<a<∞,0<b<∞formulae-sequence0𝑎0𝑏0<a<\infty,0<b<\infty for Fechner’s model. The variance of the error Z𝑍Z was set to 111 in both models. With these values, the difference in ∫Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θsubscriptΘ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃\int_{\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta is 3.804, which is non-negligible for small samples. Thus, Stevens’ model contains more distinguishable distributions than Fechner’s, and is better able to capture random noise in the data – as ?] already speculated almost 30 years ago. Experiments suggest that for regression models such as Stevens’ and Fechner’s’, as well as for Markov models and general exponential families, the approximation (2.24) is reasonably accurate already for small samples. But this is certainly not true for general models:  
  The Asymptotic Expansion of COMPnsubscriptCOMP𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n} Should Be Used with Care! (2.24) does not hold for all parametric models; and for some models for which it does hold, the o​(1)𝑜1o(1) term may only converge to 00 only for quite large sample sizes. Foster and Stine [?, ?] show that the approximation (2.24) is, in general, only valid if k𝑘k is much smaller than n𝑛n.    

Two-part codes and COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M})

We now have a clear guiding principle (minimax regret) which we can use to construct ‘optimal’ two-part codes, that achieve the minimax regret among all two-part codes. How do such optimal two-part codes compare to the NML codelength? Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be a k𝑘k-dimensional model. By slightly adjusting the arguments of [1991, Appendix], one can show that, under regularity conditions, the minimax optimal two-part code P¯2-psubscript¯𝑃2-p\bar{P}_{\text{2-p}} achieves regret

−log⁡P¯2-p​(xn∣ℳ)+log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))=k2​log⁡n2​π+log​∫θ∈Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θ+f​(k)+o​(1),subscript¯𝑃2-pconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑘2𝑛2𝜋subscript𝜃Θ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃𝑓𝑘𝑜1-\log\bar{P}_{\text{2-p}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M})+\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))=\frac{k}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}+\log\int_{\theta\in\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta+f(k)+o(1),

where f:ℕ→ℝ:𝑓→ℕℝf:{\mathbb{N}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is a bounded positive function satisfying limk→∞f​(k)=0subscript→𝑘𝑓𝑘0\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}f(k)=0. Thus, for large k𝑘k, optimally designed two-part codes are about as good as NML. The problem with two-part code MDL is that in practice, people often use much cruder codes with much larger minimax regret.

2.6.3 Bayesian Interpretation

The Bayesian method of statistical inference provides several alternative approaches to model selection. The most popular of these is based on Bayes factors [1995]. The Bayes factor method is very closely related to the refined MDL approach. Assuming uniform priors on models ℳ(1)superscriptℳ1{\cal M}^{(1)} and ℳ(2)superscriptℳ2{\cal M}^{(2)}, it tells us to select the model with largest marginal likelihood P¯Bayes​(xn∣ℳ(j))subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(j)}), where P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is as in (2.13), with the sum replaced by an integral, and w(j)superscript𝑤𝑗w^{(j)} is the density of the prior distribution on ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)}:

P¯Bayes​(xn∣ℳ(j))=∫P​(xn∣θ)​w(j)​(θ)​𝑑θ.subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptℳ𝑗𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃superscript𝑤𝑗𝜃differential-d𝜃\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(j)})=\int P(x^{n}\mid\theta)w^{(j)}(\theta)d\theta. (2.25)
ℳℳ{\cal M} is Exponential Family

Let now P¯Bayes=P¯Bayes(⋅∣ℳ)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}=\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}) for some fixed model ℳℳ{\cal M}. Under regularity conditions on ℳℳ{\cal M}, we can perform a Laplace approximation of the integral in (2.13). For the special case that ℳℳ{\cal M} is an exponential family, we obtain the following expression for the regret [1961, 1978, 1995, 1997]:

−log⁡P¯Bayes​(xn)−[−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))]=k2​log⁡n2​π−log⁡w​(θ^)+log⁡|I​(θ^)|+o​(1).subscript¯𝑃Bayessuperscript𝑥𝑛delimited-[]𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑘2𝑛2𝜋𝑤^𝜃𝐼^𝜃𝑜1-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n})-[-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))]=\frac{k}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}-\log w(\hat{\theta})+\log\sqrt{{|I(\hat{\theta})|}}+o(1). (2.26)

Let us compare this with (2.24). Under the regularity conditions needed for (2.24), the quantity on the right of (2.26) is within O​(1)𝑂1O(1) of COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}). Thus, the code length achieved with P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is within a constant of the minimax optimal −log⁡P¯nml​(xn)subscript¯𝑃nmlsuperscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}). Since −log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n})) increases linearly in n𝑛n, this means that if we compare two models ℳ(1)superscriptℳ1{\cal M}^{(1)} and ℳ(2)superscriptℳ2{\cal M}^{(2)}, then for large enough n𝑛n, Bayes and refined MDL select the same model. If we equip the Bayesian universal model with a special prior known as the Jeffreys-Bernardo prior [1946, 1994],

wJeffreys​(θ)=|I​(θ)|∫θ∈Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θ,subscript𝑤Jeffreys𝜃𝐼𝜃subscript𝜃Θ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃w_{\text{Jeffreys}}(\theta)=\frac{\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}}{\int_{\theta\in\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta}, (2.27)

then Bayes and refined NML become even more closely related: plugging in (2.27) into (2.26), we find that the right-hand side of (2.26) now simply coincides with (2.24). A concrete example of Jeffreys’ prior is given in Example 2.19. Jeffreys introduced his prior as a ‘least informative prior’, to be used when no useful prior knowledge about the parameters is available [1946]. As one may expect from such a prior, it is invariant under continuous 1-to-1 reparameterizations of the parameter space. The present analysis shows that, when ℳℳ{\cal M} is an exponential family, then it also leads to asymptotically minimax codelength regret: for large n𝑛n, refined NML model selection becomes indistinguishable from Bayes factor model selection with Jeffreys’ prior.

ℳℳ{\cal M} is not an Exponential Family

Under weak conditions on ℳℳ{\cal M}, ΘΘ\Theta and the sequence xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}, we get the following generalization of (2.26):

−log⁡P¯Bayes​(xn∣ℳ)=−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))+k2​log⁡n2​π−log⁡w​(θ^)+log⁡|I^​(xn)|+o​(1).subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳ𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑘2𝑛2𝜋𝑤^𝜃^𝐼superscript𝑥𝑛𝑜1-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M})=\\ -\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))+\frac{k}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}-\log w(\hat{\theta})+\log\sqrt{\bigl{|}\hat{I}(x^{n})\bigr{|}}+o(1). (2.28)

Here I^​(xn)^𝐼superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{I}(x^{n}) is the so-called observed information, sometimes also called observed Fisher information; see [1997] for a definition. If ℳℳ{\cal M} is an exponential family, then the observed Fisher information at xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} coincides with the Fisher information at θ^​(xn)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\theta}(x^{n}), leading to (2.26). If ℳℳ{\cal M} is not exponential, then if data are distributed according to one of the distributions in ℳℳ{\cal M}, the observed Fisher information still converges with probability 1 to the expected Fisher information. If ℳℳ{\cal M} is neither exponential, nor are the data actually generated by a distribution in ℳℳ{\cal M}, then there may be O​(1)𝑂1O(1)-discrepancies between −log⁡P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} and −log⁡P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} even for large n𝑛n.

2.6.4 Prequential Interpretation

Distributions as Prediction Strategies

Let P𝑃P be a distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n}. Applying the definition of conditional probability, we can write for every xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}:

P​(xn)=∏i=1nP​(xi)P​(xi−1)=∏i=1nP​(xi∣xi−1),𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑖1𝑛𝑃superscript𝑥𝑖𝑃superscript𝑥𝑖1superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑖1𝑛𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(x^{n})=\prod_{i=1}^{n}\frac{P(x^{i})}{P(x^{i-1})}=\prod_{i=1}^{n}P(x_{i}\mid x^{i-1}), (2.29)

so that also

−log⁡P​(xn)=∑i=1n−log⁡P​(xi∣xi−1)𝑃superscript𝑥𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1-\log P(x^{n})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}-\log P(x_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) (2.30)

Let us abbreviate P(Xi=⋅∣Xi−1=xi−1)P(X_{i}=\cdot\mid X^{i-1}=x^{i-1}) to P​(Xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}). P​(Xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) (capital Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i}) is the distribution (not a single number) of Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i} given xi−1superscript𝑥𝑖1x^{i-1}; P​(xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(x_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) (lower case xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}) is the probability (a single number) of actual outcome xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i} given xi−1superscript𝑥𝑖1x^{i-1}. We can think of −log⁡P​(xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1-\log P(x_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) as the loss incurred when predicting Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i} based on the conditional distribution P​(Xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}), and the actual outcome turned out to be xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}. Here ‘loss’ is measured using the so-called logarithmic score, also known simply as ‘log loss’. Note that the more likely x𝑥x is judged to be, the smaller the loss incurred when x𝑥x actually obtains. The log loss has a natural interpretation in terms of sequential gambling [1991], but its main interpretation is still in terms of coding: by (2.30), the codelength needed to encode xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} based on distribution P𝑃P is just the accumulated log loss incurred when P𝑃P is used to sequentially predict the i𝑖i-th outcome based on the past (i−1)𝑖1(i-1)-st outcomes.

(2.29) gives a fundamental re-interpretation of probability distributions as prediction strategies, mapping each individual sequence of past observations x1,…,xi−1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1x_{1},\ldots,x_{i-1} to a probabilistic prediction of the next outcome P​(Xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}). Conversely, (2.29) also shows that every probabilistic prediction strategy for sequential prediction of n𝑛n outcomes may be thought of as a probability distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n}: a strategy is identified with a function mapping all potential initial segments xi−1superscript𝑥𝑖1x^{i-1} to the prediction that is made for the next outcome Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i}, after having seen xi−1superscript𝑥𝑖1x^{i-1}. Thus, it is a function S:∪0≤i<n𝒳i→𝒫𝒳:𝑆→subscript0𝑖𝑛superscript𝒳𝑖subscript𝒫𝒳S:\cup_{0\leq i<n}{\cal X}^{i}\rightarrow{\cal P}_{\cal X}, where 𝒫𝒳subscript𝒫𝒳{\cal P}_{\cal X} is the set of distributions on 𝒳𝒳{\cal X}. We can now define, for each i<n𝑖𝑛i<n, all xi∈𝒳isuperscript𝑥𝑖superscript𝒳𝑖x^{i}\in{\cal X}^{i}, P​(Xi∣xi−1):=S​(xi−1)assign𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1𝑆superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}){:=}S(x^{i-1}). We can turn these partial distributions into a distribution on 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n} by sequentially plugging them into (2.29).

Log Loss for Universal Models

Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be some parametric model and let P¯¯𝑃\bar{P} be some universal model/code relative to ℳℳ{\cal M}. What do the individual predictions P¯​(Xi∣xi−1)¯𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1\bar{P}(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) look like? Readers familiar with Bayesian statistics will realize that for i.i.d. models, the Bayesian predictive distribution P¯Bayes​(Xi∣xi−1)subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) converges to the ML distribution P(⋅∣θ^(xi−1))P(\cdot\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1})); Example 2.19 provides a concrete case. It seems reasonable to assume that something similar holds not just for P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} but for universal models in general. This in turn suggests that we may approximate the conditional distributions P¯​(Xi∣xi−1)¯𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1\bar{P}(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) of any ‘good’ universal model by the maximum likelihood predictions P(⋅∣θ^(xi−1))P(\cdot\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1})). Indeed, we can recursively define the ‘maximum likelihood plug-in’ distribution P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} by setting, for i=1𝑖1i=1 to n𝑛n,

P¯plug-in(Xi=⋅∣xi−1):=P(X=⋅∣θ^(xi−1)).\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(X_{i}=\cdot\mid x^{i-1}){:=}P(X=\cdot\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1})). (2.31)

Then

−log⁡P¯plug-in​(xn):=∑i=1n−log⁡P​(xi∣θ^​(xi−1)).assignsubscript¯𝑃plug-insuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑥𝑖^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖1-\log\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(x^{n}){:=}\sum_{i=1}^{n}-\log P(x_{i}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1})). (2.32)

Indeed, it turns out that under regularity conditions on ℳℳ{\cal M} and xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n},

−log⁡P¯plug-in​(xn)=−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))+k2​log⁡n+O​(1),subscript¯𝑃plug-insuperscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑘2𝑛𝑂1-\log\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(x^{n})=-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))+\frac{k}{2}\log n+O(1), (2.33)

showing that P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} acts as a universal model relative to ℳℳ{\cal M}, its performance being within a constant of the minimax optimal P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}. The construction of P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} can be easily extended to non-i.i.d. models, and then, under regularity conditions, (2.33) still holds; we omit the details.

  • We note that all general proofs of (2.33) that we are aware of show that (2.33) holds with probability 1 or in expectation for sequences generated by some distribution in ℳℳ{\cal M} [1984, 1986, 1989]. Note that the expressions (2.24) and (2.28) for the regret of P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} and P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} hold for a much wider class of sequences; they also hold with probability 1 for i.i.d. sequences generated by sufficiently regular distributions outside ℳℳ{\cal M}. Not much is known about the regret obtained by P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} for such sequences, except for some special cases such as if ℳℳ{\cal M} is the Gaussian model.

In general, there is no need to use the ML estimator θ^​(xi−1)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖1\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1}) in the definition (2.31). Instead, we may try some other estimator which asymptotically converges to the ML estimator – it turns out that some estimators considerably outperform the ML estimator in the sense that (2.32) becomes a much better approximation of −log⁡P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, see Example 2.19. Irrespective of whether we use the ML estimator or something else, we call model selection based on (2.32) the prequential form of MDL in honor of A.P. Dawid’s ‘prequential analysis’, Section 2.9. It is also known as ‘predictive MDL’. The validity of (2.33) was discovered independently by Rissanen [?] and Dawid [?].

The prequential view gives us a fourth interpretation of refined MDL model selection: given models ℳ(1)superscriptℳ1{\cal M}^{(1)} and ℳ(2)superscriptℳ2{\cal M}^{(2)}, MDL tells us to pick the model that minimizes the accumulated prediction error resulting from sequentially predicting future outcomes given all the past outcomes.

Example 2.18

[GLRT and Prequential Model Selection] How does this differ from the naive version of the generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) that we introduced in Example 2.15? In GLRT, we associate with each model the log-likelihood (minus log loss) that can be obtained by the ML estimator. This is the predictor within the model that minimizes log loss with hindsight, after having seen the data. In contrast, prequential model selection associates with each model the log-likelihood (minus log loss) that can be obtained by using a sequence of ML estimators θ^​(xi−1)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖1\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1}) to predict data xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i}. Crucially, the data on which ML estimators are evaluated has not been used in constructing the ML estimators themselves. This makes the prediction scheme ‘honest’ (different data are used for training and testing) and explains why it automatically protects us against overfitting.

Example 2.19

[Laplace and Jeffreys] Consider the prequential distribution for the Bernoulli model, Example 2.7, defined as in (2.31). We show that if we take θ^^𝜃\hat{\theta} in (2.31) equal to the ML estimator n[1]/nsubscript𝑛delimited-[]1𝑛n_{[1]}/n, then the resulting P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} is not a universal model; but a slight modification of the ML estimator makes P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} a very good universal model. Suppose that n≥3𝑛3n\geq 3 and (x1,x2,x3)=(0,0,1)subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3001(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3})=(0,0,1) – a not-so-unlikely initial segment according to most θ𝜃\theta. Then P¯plug-in​(X3=1∣x1,x2)=P​(X=1∣θ^​(x1,x2))=0subscript¯𝑃plug-insubscript𝑋3conditional1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2𝑃𝑋conditional1^𝜃subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥20\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(X_{3}=1\mid x_{1},x_{2})=P(X=1\mid\hat{\theta}(x_{1},x_{2}))=0, so that by (2.32),
