−log⁡P¯plug-in​(xn)≥−log⁡P¯plug-in​(x3∣x1,x2)=∞,subscript¯𝑃plug-insuperscript𝑥𝑛subscript¯𝑃plug-inconditionalsubscript𝑥3subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2-\log\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(x^{n})\geq-\log\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(x_{3}\mid x_{1},x_{2})=\infty,

whence P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} is not universal. Now let us consider the modified ML estimator

θ^λ​(xn):=n[1]+λn+2​λ.assignsubscript^𝜃𝜆superscript𝑥𝑛subscript𝑛delimited-[]1𝜆𝑛2𝜆\hat{\theta}_{\lambda}(x^{n}){:=}\frac{n_{[1]}+\lambda}{n+2\lambda}. (2.34)

If we take λ=0𝜆0\lambda=0, we get the ordinary ML estimator. If we take λ=1𝜆1\lambda=1, then an exercise involving beta-integrals shows that, for all i,xi𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖i,x^{i}, P​(Xi∣θ^1​(xi−1))=P¯Bayes​(Xi∣xi−1)𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖subscript^𝜃1superscript𝑥𝑖1subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid\hat{\theta}_{1}(x^{i-1}))=\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}), where P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} is defined relative to the uniform prior w​(θ)≡1𝑤𝜃1w(\theta)\equiv 1. Thus θ^1​(xi−1)subscript^𝜃1superscript𝑥𝑖1\hat{\theta}_{1}(x^{i-1}) corresponds to the Bayesian predictive distribution for the uniform prior. This prediction rule was advocated by the great probabilist P.S. de Laplace, co-originator of Bayesian statistics. It may be interpreted as ML estimation based on an extended sample, containing some ‘virtual’ data: an extra 00 and an extra 111.

Even better, a similar calculation shows that if we take λ=1/2𝜆12\lambda=1/2, the resulting estimator is equal to P¯Bayes​(Xi∣xi−1)subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝑥𝑖1\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(X_{i}\mid x^{i-1}) defined relative to Jeffreys’ prior. Asymptotically, P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} with Jeffreys’ prior achieves the same codelengths as P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} (Section 2.6.3). It follows that P¯plug-insubscript¯𝑃plug-in\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}} with the slightly modified ML estimator is asymptotically indistinguishable from the optimal universal model P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}!

For more general models ℳℳ{\cal M}, such simple modifications of the ML estimator usually do not correspond to a Bayesian predictive distribution; for example, if ℳℳ{\cal M} is not convex (closed under taking mixtures) then a point estimator (an element of ℳℳ{\cal M}) typically does not correspond to the Bayesian predictive distribution (a mixture of elements of ℳℳ{\cal M}). Nevertheless, modifying the ML estimator by adding some virtual data y1,…,ymsubscript𝑦1…subscript𝑦𝑚y_{1},\ldots,y_{m} and replacing P​(Xi∣θ^​(xi−1))𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖1P(X_{i}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1})) by P​(Xi∣θ^​(xi−1,ym))𝑃conditionalsubscript𝑋𝑖^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖1superscript𝑦𝑚P(X_{i}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{i-1},y^{m})) in the definition (2.31) may still lead to good universal models. This is of great practical importance, since, using (2.32), −log⁡P¯plug-in​(xn)subscript¯𝑃plug-insuperscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}_{\text{plug-in}}(x^{n}) is often much easier to compute than −log⁡P¯Bayes​(xn)subscript¯𝑃Bayessuperscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(x^{n}).

Summary

We introduced the refined MDL Principle for model selection in a restricted setting. Refined MDL amounts to selecting the model under which the data achieve the smallest stochastic complexity, which is the codelength according to the minimax optimal universal model. We gave an asymptotic expansion of stochastic and parametric complexity, and interpreted these concepts in four different ways.

2.7 General Refined MDL: Gluing it All Together

In the previous section we introduced a ‘refined’ MDL principle based on minimax regret. Unfortunately, this principle can be applied only in very restricted settings. We now show how to extend refined MDL, leading to a general MDL Principle, applicable to a wide variety of model selection problems. In doing so we glue all our previous insights (including ‘crude MDL’) together, thereby uncovering a single general, underlying principle, formulated in Figure 2.4. Therefore, if one understands the material in this section, then one understands the Minimum Description Length Principle.

First, Section 2.7.1, we show how to compare infinitely many models. Then, Section 2.7.2, we show how to proceed for models ℳℳ{\cal M} for which the parametric complexity is undefined. Remarkably, a single, general idea resides behind our solution of both problems, and this leads us to formulate, in Section 2.7.3, a single, general refined MDL Principle.

2.7.1 Model Selection with Infinitely Many Models

Suppose we want to compare more than two models for the same data. If the number to be compared is finite, we can proceed as before and pick the model ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} with smallest −log⁡P¯nml​(xn∣ℳ(k))subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(k)}). If the number of models is infinite, we have to be more careful. Say we compare models ℳ(1),ℳ(2),…superscriptℳ1superscriptℳ2…{\cal M}^{(1)},{\cal M}^{(2)},\ldots for data xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. We may be tempted to pick the model minimizing −log⁡P¯nml​(xn∣ℳ(k))subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(k)}) over all k∈{1,2,…}𝑘12…k\in\{1,2,\ldots\}, but in some cases this gives unintended results. To illustrate, consider the extreme case that every ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} contains just one distribution. For example, let ℳ(1)={P1},ℳ(2)={P2},…formulae-sequencesuperscriptℳ1subscript𝑃1superscriptℳ2subscript𝑃2…{\cal M}^{(1)}=\{P_{1}\},{\cal M}^{(2)}=\{P_{2}\},\ldots where {P1,P2,…}subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2…\{P_{1},P_{2},\ldots\} is the set of all Markov chains with rational-valued parameters. In that case, COMPn​(ℳ(k))=0subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘0\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(k)})=0 for all k𝑘k, and we would always select the maximum likelihood Markov chain that assigns probability 1 to data xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. Typically this will be a chain of very high order, severely overfitting the data. This cannot be right! A better idea is to pick the model minimizing

−log⁡P¯nml​(xn∣ℳ(k))+L​(k),subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘𝐿𝑘-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(k)})+L(k), (2.35)

where L𝐿L is the codelength function of some code for encoding model indices k𝑘k. We would typically choose the standard prior for the integers, L​(k)=2​log⁡k+1𝐿𝑘2𝑘1L(k)=2\log k+1, Example 2.4. By using (2.35) we avoid the overfitting problem mentioned above: if ℳ(1)={P1},ℳ(2)={P2},…formulae-sequencesuperscriptℳ1subscript𝑃1superscriptℳ2subscript𝑃2…{\cal M}^{(1)}=\{P_{1}\},{\cal M}^{(2)}=\{P_{2}\},\ldots where P1,P2,…subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2…P_{1},P_{2},\ldots is a list of all the rational-parameter Markov chains, (2.35) would reduce to two-part code MDL (Section 2.4) which is asymptotically consistent. On the other hand, if ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} represents the set of k𝑘k-th order Markov chains, the term L​(k)𝐿𝑘L(k) is typically negligible compared to COMPn​(ℳ(k))subscriptCOMP𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}^{(k)}), the complexity term associated with ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} that is hidden in −log⁡P¯nml​(ℳ(k))subscript¯𝑃nmlsuperscriptℳ𝑘-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}({\cal M}^{(k)}): thus, the complexity of ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} comes from the fact that for large k𝑘k, ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} contains many distinguishable distributions; not from the much smaller term L​(k)≈2​log⁡k𝐿𝑘2𝑘L(k)\approx 2\log k.

To make our previous approach for a finite set of models compatible with (2.35), we can reinterpret it as follows: we assign uniform codelengths (a uniform prior) to the ℳ(1),…,ℳ(M)superscriptℳ1…superscriptℳ𝑀{\cal M}^{(1)},\ldots,{\cal M}^{(M)} under consideration, so that for k=1,…,M𝑘1…𝑀k=1,\ldots,M, L​(k)=log⁡M𝐿𝑘𝑀L(k)=\log M. We then pick the model minimizing (2.35). Since L​(k)𝐿𝑘L(k) is constant over k𝑘k, it plays no role in the minimization and can be dropped from the equation, so that our procedure reduces to our original refined MDL model selection method. We shall henceforth assume that we always encode the model index, either implicitly (if the number of models is finite) or explicitly. The general principle behind this is explained in Section 2.7.3.

2.7.2 The Infinity Problem

For some of the most commonly used models, the parametric complexity COMP​(ℳ)COMPℳ\text{\bf COMP}({\cal M}) is undefined. A prime example is the Gaussian location model, which we discuss below. As we will see, we can ‘repair’ the situation using the same general idea as in the previous subsection.

Example 2.20

Parametric Complexity of the Normal Distributions Let ℳℳ{\cal M} be the family of Normal distributions with fixed variance σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2} and varying mean μ𝜇\mu, identified by their densities

P​(x|μ)=12​π​σ​e−(x−μ)22​σ2,𝑃conditional𝑥𝜇12𝜋𝜎superscript𝑒superscript𝑥𝜇22superscript𝜎2P(x|\mu)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}},

extended to sequences x1,…,xnsubscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛x_{1},\ldots,x_{n} by taking product densities. As is well-known [1990], the ML estimator μ^​(xn)^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\mu}(x^{n}) is equal to the sample mean: μ^​(xn)=n−1​∑i=1nxi^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝑛1superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛subscript𝑥𝑖\hat{\mu}(x^{n})=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{i}. An easy calculation shows that

COMPn​(ℳ)=∫xnP​(xn∣μ^​(xn))​𝑑xn=∞,subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳsubscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛differential-dsuperscript𝑥𝑛\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M})=\int_{x^{n}}P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\mu}(x^{n}))dx^{n}=\infty,

where we abbreviated d​x1​…​d​xn𝑑subscript𝑥1…𝑑subscript𝑥𝑛dx_{1}\ldots dx_{n} to d​xn𝑑superscript𝑥𝑛dx^{n}. Therefore, we cannot use basic MDL model selection. It also turns out that I​(μ)=σ−2𝐼𝜇superscript𝜎2I(\mu)=\sigma^{-2} so that

∫Θ|I​(θ)|​𝑑θ=∫μ∈ℝ|I​(μ)|​𝑑μ=∞.subscriptΘ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃subscript𝜇ℝ𝐼𝜇differential-d𝜇\int_{\Theta}\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta=\int_{\mu\in{\mathbb{R}}}\sqrt{|I(\mu)|}d\mu=\infty.

Thus, the Bayesian universal model approach with Jeffreys’ prior cannot be applied either. Does this mean that our MDL model selection and complexity definitions break down even in such a simple case? Luckily, it turns out that they can be repaired, as we now show. ?] and ?] show that, for all intervals [a,b]𝑎𝑏[a,b],

∫xn:μ^​(xn)∈[a,b]P​(xn∣μ^​(xn))​𝑑xn=b−a2​π​σ⋅n.subscript:superscript𝑥𝑛^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛𝑎𝑏𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛differential-dsuperscript𝑥𝑛⋅𝑏𝑎2𝜋𝜎𝑛\int_{x^{n}:\hat{\mu}(x^{n})\in[a,b]}P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\mu}(x^{n}))dx^{n}=\frac{b-a}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\cdot\sqrt{n}. (2.36)

Suppose for the moment that it is known that μ^^𝜇\hat{\mu} lies in some set [−K,K]𝐾𝐾[-K,K] for some fixed K𝐾K. Let ℳKsubscriptℳ𝐾{\cal M}_{K} be the set of conditional distributions thus obtained: ℳK={P′(⋅∣μ)∣μ∈ℝ}{\cal M}_{K}=\{P^{\prime}(\cdot\mid\mu)\mid\mu\in{\mathbb{R}}\}, where P′​(xn∣μ)superscript𝑃′conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜇P^{\prime}(x^{n}\mid\mu) is the density of xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} according to the normal distribution with mean μ𝜇\mu, conditioned on |n−1​∑xi|≤Ksuperscript𝑛1subscript𝑥𝑖𝐾|n^{-1}\sum x_{i}|\leq K. By (2.36), the ‘conditional’ minimax regret distribution P¯nml(⋅∣ℳK)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}_{K}) is well-defined for all K>0𝐾0K>0. That is, for all xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} with |μ^​(xn)|≤K^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛𝐾|\hat{\mu}(x^{n})|\leq K,

P¯nml​(xn∣ℳK)=P′​(xn∣μ^​(xn))∫xn:|μ^​(xn)|<KP′(xn∣μ^(xn))dxn,\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}_{K})=\frac{P^{\prime}(x^{n}\mid\hat{\mu}(x^{n}))}{\int_{x^{n}\;:\;|\hat{\mu}(x^{n})|<K}P^{\prime}(x^{n}\mid\hat{\mu}(x^{n}))dx^{n},}

with regret (or ‘conditional’ complexity),

COMPn​(ℳK)=log​∫|μ^​(xn)|<KP′​(xn∣μ^​(xn))​𝑑xn=log⁡K+12​log⁡n2​π−log⁡σ+1.subscriptCOMP𝑛subscriptℳ𝐾subscript^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛𝐾superscript𝑃′conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛differential-dsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝐾12𝑛2𝜋𝜎1\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}_{K})=\log\int_{|\hat{\mu}(x^{n})|<K}P^{\prime}(x^{n}\mid\hat{\mu}(x^{n}))dx^{n}=\log K+\frac{1}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}-\log\sigma+1.

This suggests to redefine the complexity of the full model ℳℳ{\cal M} so that its regret depends on the area in which μ^^𝜇\hat{\mu} falls. The most straightforward way of achieving this is to define a meta-universal model for ℳℳ{\cal M}, combining the NML with a two-part code: we encode data by first encoding some value for K𝐾K. We then encode the actual data xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} using the code P¯nml(⋅|ℳK)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot|{\cal M}_{K}). The resulting code P¯metasubscript¯𝑃meta\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}} is a universal code for ℳℳ{\cal M} with lengths

−log⁡P¯meta​(xn|ℳ):=minK⁡{−log⁡P¯meta​(xn∣ℳK)+L​(K)}.assignsubscript¯𝑃metaconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳsubscript𝐾subscript¯𝑃metaconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscriptℳ𝐾𝐿𝐾-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}(x^{n}|{\cal M}){:=}\min_{K}\ \bigl{\{}-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}_{K})+L(K)\bigr{\}}. (2.37)

The idea is now to base MDL model selection on P¯meta(⋅|ℳ)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}(\cdot|{\cal M}) as in (2.37) rather than on the (undefined) P¯nml(⋅|ℳ)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot|{\cal M}). To make this work, we need to choose L𝐿L in a clever manner. A good choice is to encode K′=log⁡Ksuperscript𝐾′𝐾K^{\prime}=\log K as an integer, using the standard code for the integers. To see why, note that the regret of P¯metasubscript¯𝑃meta\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}} now becomes:

−log⁡P¯meta​(xn∣ℳ)−[−log⁡P​(xn∣μ^​(xn))]=minK:log⁡K∈{1,2,…}⁡{log⁡K+12​log⁡n2​π−log⁡σ+1+2​log⁡⌈log⁡K⌉}+1≤log⁡|μ^​(xn)|+2​log⁡log⁡|μ^​(xn)|+12​log⁡n2​π−log⁡σ+4≤COMPn​(ℳ|μ^|)+2​log⁡COMPn​(ℳ|μ^|)+3.subscript¯𝑃metaconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳdelimited-[]𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛subscript:𝐾𝐾12…𝐾12𝑛2𝜋𝜎12𝐾1^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛2^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛12𝑛2𝜋𝜎4subscriptCOMP𝑛subscriptℳ^𝜇2subscriptCOMP𝑛subscriptℳ^𝜇3-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M})-[-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\mu}(x^{n}))]=\\ \min_{K:\log K\in\{1,2,\ldots\}}\ \bigl{\{}\log K+\frac{1}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}-\log\sigma+1+2\log\lceil\log K\rceil\ \bigr{\}}+1\leq\\ \log|\hat{\mu}(x^{n})|+2\log\log|\hat{\mu}(x^{n})|+\frac{1}{2}\log\frac{n}{2\pi}-\log\sigma+4\leq\\ \text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}_{|\hat{\mu}|})+2\log\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}_{|\hat{\mu}|})+3. (2.38)

If we had known a good bound K𝐾K on |μ^|^𝜇|\hat{\mu}| a priori, we could have used the NML model P¯nml(⋅∣ℳK)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}_{K}). With ‘maximal’ a priori knowledge, we would have used the model P¯nml(⋅∣ℳ|μ^|)\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}_{|\hat{\mu}|}), leading to regret COMPn​(ℳ|μ^|)subscriptCOMP𝑛subscriptℳ^𝜇\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}_{|\hat{\mu}|}). The regret achieved by P¯metasubscript¯𝑃meta\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}} is almost as good as this ‘smallest possible regret-with-hindsight’ COMPn​(ℳ|μ^|)subscriptCOMP𝑛subscriptℳ^𝜇\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}_{|\hat{\mu}|}): the difference is much smaller than, in fact logarithmic in, COMPn​(ℳ|μ^|)subscriptCOMP𝑛subscriptℳ^𝜇\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}_{|\hat{\mu}|}) itself, no matter what xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} we observe. This is the underlying reason why we choose to encode K𝐾K with log-precision: the basic idea in refined MDL was to minimize worst-case regret, or additional code-length compared to the code that achieves the minimal code-length with hindsight. Here, we use this basic idea on a meta-level: we design a code such that the additional regret is minimized, compared to the code that achieves the minimal regret with hindsight.

This meta-two-part coding idea was introduced by ?]. It can be extended to a wide range of models with COMPn​(ℳ)=∞subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M})=\infty; for example, if the Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i} represent outcomes of a Poisson or geometric distribution, one can encode a bound on μ𝜇\mu just like in Example 2.20. If ℳℳ{\cal M} is the full Gaussian model with both μ𝜇\mu and σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2} allowed to vary, one has to encode a bound on μ^^𝜇\hat{\mu} and a bound on σ^2superscript^𝜎2\hat{\sigma}^{2}. Essentially the same holds for linear regression problems, Section 2.8.

Renormalized Maximum Likelihood

Meta-two-part coding is just one possible solution to the problem of undefined COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}). It is suboptimal, the main reason being the use of 2-part codes. Indeed, these 2-part codes are not complete (Section 2.2): they reserve several codewords for the same data D=(x1,…,xn)𝐷subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛D=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}) (one for each integer value of log⁡K𝐾\log K); therefore, there must exist more efficient (one-part) codes P¯meta′superscriptsubscript¯𝑃meta′\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}^{\prime} such that for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n}, P¯meta′​(xn)>P¯meta​(xn)superscriptsubscript¯𝑃meta′superscript𝑥𝑛subscript¯𝑃metasuperscript𝑥𝑛\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}^{\prime}(x^{n})>\bar{P}_{\text{\rm meta}}(x^{n}); in keeping with the idea that we should minimize description length, such alternative codes are preferable. This realization has led to a search for more efficient and intrinsic solutions to the problem. ?] consider the possibility of restricting the parameter values rather than the data, and develop a general framework for comparing universal codes for models with undefined COMP​(ℳ)COMPℳ\text{\bf COMP}({\cal M}). ?] suggests the following elegant solution. He defines the Renormalized Maximum Likelihood (RNML) distribution P¯rnmlsubscript¯𝑃rnml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm rnml}}. In our Gaussian example, this universal model would be defined as follows. Let K^​(xn)^𝐾superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{K}(x^{n}) be the bound on μ^​(xn)^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{\mu}(x^{n}) that maximizes P¯nml​(xn∣ℳK)subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscriptℳ𝐾\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}_{K}) for the actually given K𝐾K. That is, K^​(xn)=|μ^​(xn)|^𝐾superscript𝑥𝑛^𝜇superscript𝑥𝑛\hat{K}(x^{n})=|\hat{\mu}(x^{n})|. Then P¯rnmlsubscript¯𝑃rnml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm rnml}} is defined as, for all xn∈𝒳nsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n},

P¯rnml​(xn|ℳ)=P¯nml​(xn|ℳK^​(xn))∫xn∈ℝnP¯nml​(xn∣ℳK^​(xn))​𝑑xn.subscript¯𝑃rnmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳsubscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscriptℳ^𝐾superscript𝑥𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscriptℝ𝑛subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛subscriptℳ^𝐾superscript𝑥𝑛differential-dsuperscript𝑥𝑛\bar{P}_{\text{\rm rnml}}(x^{n}|{\cal M})=\frac{\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}|{\cal M}_{\hat{K}(x^{n})})}{\int_{x^{n}\in{\mathbb{R}}^{n}}\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}_{\hat{K}(x^{n})})dx^{n}}. (2.39)

Model selection between a finite set of models now proceeds by selecting the model maximizing the re-normalized likelihood (2.39).

Region Indifference

All the approaches considered thus far slightly prefer some regions of the parameter space over others. In spite of its elegance, even the Rissanen renormalization is slightly ‘arbitrary’ in this way: had we chosen the origin of the real line differently, the same sequence xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} would have achieved a different codelength −log⁡P¯rnml​(xn∣ℳ)subscript¯𝑃rnmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳ-\log\bar{P}_{\text{\rm rnml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}). In recent work, Liang and Barron [?, ?] consider a novel and quite different approach for dealing with infinite COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) that partially addresses this problem. They make use of the fact that, while Jeffreys’ prior is improper (∫|I​(θ)|​𝑑θ𝐼𝜃differential-d𝜃\int\sqrt{|I(\theta)|}d\theta is infinite), using Bayes’ rule we can still compute Jeffreys’ posterior based on the first few observations, and this posterior turns out to be a proper probability measure after all. Liang and Barron use universal models of a somewhat different type than P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, so it remains to be investigated whether their approach can be adapted to the form of MDL discussed here.

2.7.3 The General Picture

Section 2.7.1 illustrates that, in all applications of MDL, we first define a single universal model that allows us to code all sequences with length equal to the given sample size. If the set of models is finite, we use the uniform prior. We do this in order to be as ‘honest’ as possible, treating all models under consideration on the same footing. But if the set of models becomes infinite, there exists no uniform prior any more. Therefore, we must choose a non-uniform prior/non-fixed length code to encode the model index. In order to treat all models still ‘as equally as possible’, we should use some code which is ‘close’ to uniform, in the sense that the codelength increases only very slowly with k𝑘k. We choose the standard prior for the integers (Example 2.4), but we could also have chosen different priors, for example, a prior P​(k)𝑃𝑘P(k) which is uniform on k=1..Mk=1..M for some large M𝑀M, and P​(k)∝k−2proportional-to𝑃𝑘superscript𝑘2P(k)\propto k^{-2} for k>M𝑘𝑀k>M. Whatever prior we choose, we are forced to encode a slight preference of some models over others; see Section 2.10.1.

Section 2.7.2 applies the same idea, but implemented at a meta-level: we try to associate with ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} a code for encoding outcomes in 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n} that achieves uniform (= minimax) regret for every sequence xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n}. If this is not possible, we still try to assign regret as ‘uniformly’ as we can, by carving up the parameter space in regions with larger and larger minimax regret, and devising a universal code that achieves regret not much larger than the minimax regret achievable within the smallest region containing the ML estimator. Again, the codes we used encoded a slight preference of some regions of the parameter space over others, but our aim was to keep this preference as small as possible. The general idea is summarized in Figure 2.4, which provides an (informal) definition of MDL, but only in a restricted context. If we go beyond that context, these prescriptions cannot be used literally – but extensions in the same spirit suggest themselves. Here is a first example of such an extension:

Example 2.21

[MDL and Local Maxima in the Likelihood] In practice we often work with models for which the ML estimator cannot be calculated efficiently; or at least, no algorithm for efficient calculation of the ML estimator is known. Examples are finite and Gaussian mixtures and Hidden Markov models. In such cases one typically resorts to methods such as EM or gradient descent, which find a local maximum of the likelihood surface (function) P​(xn∣θ)𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃P(x^{n}\mid\theta), leading to a local maximum likelihood estimator (LML) θ˙​(xn)˙𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\dot{\theta}(x^{n}). Suppose we need to select between a finite number of such models. We may be tempted to pick the model ℳℳ{\cal M} maximizing the normalized likelihood P¯nml​(xn∣ℳ)subscript¯𝑃nmlconditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛ℳ\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}(x^{n}\mid{\cal M}). However, if we then plan to use the local estimator θ˙​(xn)˙𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\dot{\theta}(x^{n}) for predicting future data, this is not the right thing to do. To see this, note that, if suboptimal estimators θ˙˙𝜃\dot{\theta} are to be used, the ability of model ℳℳ{\cal M} to fit arbitrary data patterns may be severely diminished! Rather than using P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, we should redefine it to take into account the fact that θ˙˙𝜃\dot{\theta} is not the global ML estimator:

P¯nml′​(xn):=P​(xn∣θ˙​(xn))∑xn∈𝒳nP​(xn∣θ˙​(xn)),assignsuperscriptsubscript¯𝑃nml′superscript𝑥𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛˙𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛˙𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}^{\prime}(x^{n}){:=}\frac{P(x^{n}\mid\dot{\theta}(x^{n}))}{\sum_{x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n}}P(x^{n}\mid\dot{\theta}(x^{n}))},

leading to an adjusted parametric complexity

COMPn′​(ℳ):=log​∑xn∈𝒳nP​(xn∣θ˙​(xn)),assignsubscriptsuperscriptCOMP′𝑛ℳsubscriptsuperscript𝑥𝑛superscript𝒳𝑛𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛˙𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛\text{\bf COMP}^{\prime}_{n}({\cal M}){:=}\log\sum_{x^{n}\in{\cal X}^{n}}P(x^{n}\mid\dot{\theta}(x^{n})), (2.40)

which, for every estimator θ˙˙𝜃\dot{\theta} different from θ^^𝜃\hat{\theta} must be strictly smaller than COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}).

Summary

We have shown how to extend refined MDL beyond the restricted settings of Section 2.6. This uncovered the general principle behind refined MDL for model selection, given in Figure 2.4. General as it may be, it only applies to model selection – in the next section we briefly discuss extensions to other applications.

2.8 Beyond Parametric Model Selection

The general principle as given in Figure 2.4 only applies to model selection. It can be extended in several directions. These range over many different tasks of inductive inference – we mention prediction, transduction (as defined in [1998]), clustering [2004] and similarity detection [2003]. In these areas there has been less research and a ‘definite’ MDL approach has not yet been formulated.

MDL has been developed in some detail for some other inductive tasks: non-parametric inference, parameter estimation and regression and classification problems. We give a very brief overview of these - for details we refer to [1998, 2001] and, for the classification case, [2004].

Non-Parametric Inference

Sometimes the model class ℳℳ{\cal M} is so large that it cannot be finitely parameterized. For example, let 𝒳=[0,1]𝒳01{\cal X}=[0,1] be the unit interval and let ℳℳ{\cal M} be the i.i.d. model consisting of all distributions on 𝒳𝒳{\cal X} with densities f𝑓f such that −log⁡f​(x)𝑓𝑥-\log f(x) is a continuous function on 𝒳𝒳{\cal X}. ℳℳ{\cal M} is clearly ‘non-parametric’: it cannot be meaningfully parameterized by a connected finite-dimensional parameter set Θ(k)⊆ℝksuperscriptΘ𝑘superscriptℝ𝑘\Theta^{(k)}\subseteq{\mathbb{R}}^{k}. We may still try to learn a distribution from ℳℳ{\cal M} in various ways, for example by histogram density estimation [1992] or kernel density estimation [1989]. MDL is quite suitable for such applications, in which we typically select a density f𝑓f from a class ℳ(n)⊂ℳsuperscriptℳ𝑛ℳ{\cal M}^{(n)}\subset{\cal M}, where ℳ(n)superscriptℳ𝑛{\cal M}^{(n)} grows with n𝑛n, and every P∗∈ℳsuperscript𝑃ℳP^{*}\in{\cal M} can be arbitrarily well approximated by members of ℳ(n),ℳ(n+1),…superscriptℳ𝑛superscriptℳ𝑛1…{\cal M}^{(n)},{\cal M}^{(n+1)},\ldots in the sense that limn→∞infP∈ℳ(n)D​(P∗∥P)=0subscript→𝑛subscriptinfimum𝑃superscriptℳ𝑛𝐷conditionalsuperscript𝑃𝑃0\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\inf_{P\in{\cal M}^{(n)}}D(P^{*}\|P)=0 [1998]. Here D𝐷D is the Kullback-Leibler divergence [1991] between P∗superscript𝑃P^{*} and P𝑃P.

MDL Parameter Estimation: Three Approaches

The ‘crude’ MDL method (Section 2.4) was a means of doing model selection and parameter estimation at the same time. ‘Refined’ MDL only dealt with selection of models. If instead, or at the same time, parameter estimates are needed, they may be obtained in three different ways. Historically the first way [1989, 2001] was to simply use the refined MDL Principle to pick a parametric model ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)}, and then, within ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)}, pick the ML estimator θ^(k)superscript^𝜃𝑘\hat{\theta}^{(k)}. After all, we associate with ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} the distribution P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} with codelengths ‘as close as possible’ to those achieved by the ML estimator. This suggests that within ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)}, we should prefer the ML estimator. But upon closer inspection, Figure 2.4 suggests to use a two-part code also to select θ𝜃\theta within ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)}; namely, we should discretize the parameter space in such a way that the resulting 2-part code achieves the minimax regret among all two-part codes; we then pick the (quantized) θ𝜃\theta minimizing the two-part code length. Essentially this approach has been worked out in detail by ?]. The resulting estimators may be called two-part code MDL estimators. A third possibility is to define predictive MDL estimators such as the Laplace and Jeffreys estimators of Example 2.19; once again, these can be understood as an extension of Figure 2.4 [1998]. These second and third possibilities are more sophisticated than the first. However, if the model ℳℳ{\cal M} is finite-dimensional parametric and n𝑛n is large, then both the two-part and the predictive MDL estimators will become indistinguishable from the maximum likelihood estimators. For this reason, it has sometimes been claimed that MDL parameter estimation is just ML parameter estimation. Since for small samples, the estimates can be quite different, this statement is misleading.

Regression

In regression problems we are interested in learning how the values y1,…,ynsubscript𝑦1…subscript𝑦𝑛y_{1},\ldots,y_{n} of a regression variable Y𝑌Y depend on the values x1,…,xnsubscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛x_{1},\ldots,x_{n} of the regressor variable X𝑋X. We assume or hope that there exists some function h:𝒳→𝒴:ℎ→𝒳𝒴h:{\cal X}\rightarrow{\cal Y} so that h​(X)ℎ𝑋h(X) predicts the value Y𝑌Y reasonably well, and we want to learn such an hℎh from data. To this end, we assume a set of candidate predictors (functions) ℋℋ{\cal H}. In Example 1.2, we took ℋℋ{\cal H} to be the set of all polynomials. In the standard formulation of this problem, we take hℎh to express that

Yi=h​(Xi)+Zi,subscript𝑌𝑖ℎsubscript𝑋𝑖subscript𝑍𝑖Y_{i}=h(X_{i})+Z_{i}, (2.41)

where the Zisubscript𝑍𝑖Z_{i} are i.i.d. Gaussian random variables with mean 00 and some variance σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2}, independent of Xisubscript𝑋𝑖X_{i}. That is, we assume Gaussian noise: (2.41) implies that the conditional density of y1,…,ynsubscript𝑦1…subscript𝑦𝑛y_{1},\ldots,y_{n}, given x1,…,xnsubscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}, is equal to the product of n𝑛n Gaussian densities:

P​(yn∣xn,σ,h)=(12​π​σ)n​exp⁡(−∑i=1n(yi−h​(xi))22​σ2).𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝑥𝑛𝜎ℎsuperscript12𝜋𝜎𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖ℎsubscript𝑥𝑖22superscript𝜎2P(y^{n}\mid x^{n},\sigma,h)=\biggl{(}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\biggr{)}^{n}\exp\biggl{(}-\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-h(x_{i}))^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\biggr{)}. (2.42)

With this choice, the log-likelihood becomes a linear function of the squared error:

−log⁡P​(yn∣xn,σ,h)=12​σ2​∑i=1n(yi−h​(xi))2+n2​log⁡2​π​σ2.𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝑥𝑛𝜎ℎ12superscript𝜎2superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑦𝑖ℎsubscript𝑥𝑖2𝑛22𝜋superscript𝜎2-\log P(y^{n}\mid x^{n},\sigma,h)=\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-h(x_{i}))^{2}+\frac{n}{2}\log 2\pi\sigma^{2}. (2.43)

Let us now assume that ℋ=∪k≥1ℋ(k)ℋsubscript𝑘1superscriptℋ𝑘{\cal H}=\cup_{k\geq 1}{\cal H}^{(k)} where for each k𝑘k, ℋ(k)superscriptℋ𝑘{\cal H}^{(k)} is a set of functions h:𝒳→𝒴:ℎ→𝒳𝒴h:{\cal X}\rightarrow{\cal Y}. For example, ℋ(k)superscriptℋ𝑘{\cal H}^{(k)} may be the set of k𝑘k-th degree polynomials.

With each model ℋ(k)superscriptℋ𝑘{\cal H}^{(k)} we can associate a set of densities (2.42), one for each (h,σ2)ℎsuperscript𝜎2(h,\sigma^{2}) with h∈ℋ(k)ℎsuperscriptℋ𝑘h\in{\cal H}^{(k)} and σ2∈ℝ+superscript𝜎2superscriptℝ\sigma^{2}\in{\mathbb{R}}^{+}. Let ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} be the resulting set of conditional distributions. Each P(⋅∣h,σ2)∈ℳ(k)P(\cdot\mid h,\sigma^{2})\in{\cal M}^{(k)} is identified by the parameter vector (α0,…,αk,σ2)subscript𝛼0…subscript𝛼𝑘superscript𝜎2(\alpha_{0},\ldots,\alpha_{k},\sigma^{2}) so that h​(x):=∑j=0kαj​xjassignℎ𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑗0𝑘subscript𝛼𝑗superscript𝑥𝑗h(x){:=}\sum_{j=0}^{k}\alpha_{j}x^{j}. By Section 2.7.1, (2.9) MDL tells us to select the model minimizing

−log⁡P¯​(yn∣ℳ(k),xn)+L​(k)¯𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘superscript𝑥𝑛𝐿𝑘-\log\bar{P}(y^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(k)},x^{n})+L(k) (2.44)

where we may take L​(k)=2​log⁡k+1𝐿𝑘2𝑘1L(k)=2\log k+1, and P¯(⋅∣ℳ(k),⋅)\bar{P}(\cdot\mid{\cal M}^{(k)},\cdot) is now a conditional universal model with small minimax regret. (2.44) ignores the codelength of x1,…,xnsubscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑛x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}. Intuitively, this is because we are only interested in learning how y𝑦y depends on x𝑥x; therefore, we do not care how many bits are needed to encode x𝑥x. Formally, this may be understood as follows: we really are encoding the x𝑥x-values as well, but we do so using a fixed code that does not depend on the hypothesis hℎh under consideration. Thus, we are really trying to find the model ℳ(k)superscriptℳ𝑘{\cal M}^{(k)} minimizing

−log⁡P¯​(yn∣ℳ(k),xn)+L​(k)+L′​(xn)¯𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘superscript𝑥𝑛𝐿𝑘superscript𝐿′superscript𝑥𝑛-\log\bar{P}(y^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(k)},x^{n})+L(k)+L^{\prime}(x^{n})

where L′superscript𝐿′L^{\prime} represents some code for 𝒳nsuperscript𝒳𝑛{\cal X}^{n}. Since this codelength does not involve k𝑘k, it can be dropped from the minimization; see Figure 2.5.

We will not go into the precise definition of P¯​(yn∣ℳ(k),xn)¯𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑦𝑛superscriptℳ𝑘superscript𝑥𝑛\bar{P}(y^{n}\mid{\cal M}^{(k)},x^{n}). Ideally, it should be an NML distribution, but just as in Example 2.20, this NML distribution is not well-defined. We can get reasonable alternative universal models after all using any of the methods described in Section 2.7.2; see [1998] and [2000] for details.

‘Non-probabilistic’ Regression and Classification

In the approach we just described, we modeled the noise as being normally distributed. Alternatively, it has been tried to directly try to learn functions h∈ℋℎℋh\in{\cal H} from the data, without making any probabilistic assumptions about the noise [1989, 1990, 1998, 1998, 1999]. The idea is to learn a function hℎh that leads to good predictions of future data from the same source in the spirit of Vapnik’s [?] statistical learning theory. Here prediction quality is measured by some fixed loss function; different loss functions lead to different instantiations of the procedure. Such a version of MDL is meant to be more robust, leading to inference of a ‘good’ h∈ℋℎℋh\in{\cal H} irrespective of the details of the noise distribution. This loss-based approach has also been the method of choice in applying MDL to classification problems. Here 𝒴𝒴{\cal Y} takes on values in a finite set, and the goal is to match each feature X𝑋X (for example, a bit map of a handwritten digit) with its corresponding label or class (e.g., a digit). While several versions of MDL for classification have been proposed [1989, 1989, 1997], most of these can be reduced to the same approach based on a 0/1010/1-valued loss function [1998]. In recent work [2004] we show that this MDL approach to classification without making assumptions about the noise may behave suboptimally: we exhibit situations where no matter how large n𝑛n, MDL keeps overfitting, selecting an overly complex model with suboptimal predictive behavior. Modifications of MDL suggested by ?] and ?] do not suffer from this defect, but they do not admit a natural coding interpretation any longer. All in all, current versions of MDL that avoid probabilistic assumptions are still in their infancy, and more research is needed to find out whether they can be modified to perform well in more general and realistic settings.

Summary

In the previous sections, we have covered basic refined MDL (Section 2.6), general refined MDL (Section 2.7), and several extensions of refined MDL (this section). This concludes our technical description of refined MDL. It only remains to place MDL in its proper context: what does it do compared to other methods of inductive inference? And how well does it perform, compared to other methods? The next two sections are devoted to these questions.

2.9 Relations to Other Approaches to Inductive Inference

How does MDL compare to other model selection and statistical inference methods? In order to answer this question, we first have to be precise about what we mean by ‘MDL’; this is done in Section 2.9.1. We then continue in Section 2.9.2 by summarizing MDL’s relation to Bayesian inference, Wallace’s MML Principle, Dawid’s prequential model validation, cross-validation and an ‘idealized’ version of MDL based on Kolmogorov complexity. The literature has also established connections between MDL and Jaynes’ [?] Maximum Entropy Principle [1986, 1997, 1998, 2000, 2004] and Vapnik’s [?] structural risk minimization principle [1998], but there is no space here to discuss these. Relations between MDL and Akaike’s AIC [2002] are subtle. They are discussed by, for example, ?].

2.9.1 What is ‘MDL’?

‘MDL’ is used by different authors in somewhat different meanings. Some authors use MDL as a broad umbrella term for all types of inductive inference based on data compression. This would, for example, include the ‘idealized’ versions of MDL based on Kolmogorov complexity and Wallaces’s MML Principle, to be discussed below. On the other extreme, for historical reasons, some authors use the MDL Criterion to describe a very specific (and often not very successful) model selection criterion equivalent to BIC, discussed further below.

Here we adopt the meaning of the term that is embraced in the survey [1998], written by arguably the three most important contributors to the field: we use MDL for general inference based on universal models. These include, but are not limited to approaches in the spirit of Figure 2.4. For example, some authors have based their inferences on ‘expected’ rather than ‘individual sequence’ universal models [1998, 2004a]. Moreover, if we go beyond model selection (Section 2.8), then the ideas of Figure 2.4 have to be modified to some extent. In fact, one of the main strengths of “MDL” in this broad sense is that it can be applied to ever more exotic modeling situations, in which the models do not resemble anything that is usually encountered in statistical practice. An example is the model of context-free grammars, already suggested by ?]. In this tutorial, we call applications of MDL that strictly fit into the scheme of Figure 2.4 refined MDL for model/hypothesis selection; when we simply say ‘MDL’, we mean ‘inductive inference based on universal models’. This form of inductive inference goes hand in hand with Rissanen’s radical MDL philosophy, which views learning as finding useful properties of the data, not necessarily related to the existence of a ‘truth’ underlying the data. This view was outlined in Chapter 1, Section 1.5. Although MDL practitioners and theorists are usually sympathetic to it, the different interpretations of MDL listed in Section 2.6 make clear that MDL applications can also be justified without adopting such a radical philosophy.

2.9.2 MDL and Bayesian Inference

Bayesian statistics [1997, 1994] is one of the most well-known, frequently and successfully applied paradigms of statistical inference. It is often claimed that ‘MDL is really just a special case of Bayes19’. Although there are close similarities, this is simply not true. To see this quickly, consider the basic quantity in refined MDL: the NML distribution P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, Equation (2.19). While P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} – although defined in a completely different manner – turns out to be closely related to the Bayesian marginal likelihood, this is no longer the case for its ‘localized’ version (2.40). There is no mention of anything like this code/distribution in any Bayesian textbook! Thus, it must be the case that Bayes and MDL are somehow different.

MDL as a Maximum Probability Principle

For a more detailed analysis, we need to distinguish between the two central tenets of modern Bayesian statistics: (1) Probability distributions are used to represent uncertainty, and to serve as a basis for making predictions; rather than standing for some imagined ‘true state of nature’. (2) All inference and decision-making is done in terms of prior and posterior distributions. MDL sticks with (1) (although here the ‘distributions’ are primarily interpreted as ‘codelength functions’), but not (2): MDL allows the use of arbitrary universal models such as NML and prequential universal models; the Bayesian universal model does not have a special status among these. In this sense, Bayes offers the statistician less freedom in choice of implementation than MDL. In fact, MDL may be reinterpreted as a maximum probability principle, where the maximum is relative to some given model, in the worst-case over all sequences (Rissanen [?, ?] uses the phrase ‘global maximum likelihood principle’). Thus, whenever the Bayesian universal model is used in an MDL application, a prior should be used that minimizes worst-case codelength regret, or equivalently, maximizes worst-case relative probability. There is no comparable principle for choosing priors in Bayesian statistics, and in this respect, Bayes offers a lot more freedom than MDL.

  • Example 2.22

    There is a conceptual problem with Bayes’ use of prior distributions: in practice, we very often want to use models which we a priori know to be wrong – see Example 1.5. If we use Bayes for such models, then we are forced to put a prior distribution on a set of distributions which we know to be wrong - that is, we have degree-of-belief 111 in something we know not to be the case. From an MDL viewpoint, these priors are interpreted as tools to achieve short codelengths rather than degrees-of-belief and there is nothing strange about the situation; but from a Bayesian viewpoint, it seems awkward. To be sure, Bayesian inference often gives good results even if the model ℳℳ{\cal M} is known to be wrong; the point is that (a) if one is a strict Bayesian, one would never apply Bayesian inference to such misspecified ℳℳ{\cal M}, and (b), the Bayesian theory offers no clear explanation of why Bayesian inference might still give good results for such ℳℳ{\cal M}. MDL provides both codelength and predictive-sequential interpretations of Bayesian inference, which help explain why Bayesian inference may do something reasonable even if ℳℳ{\cal M} is misspecified. To be fair, we should add that there exists variations of the Bayesian philosophy (e.g. ?]’s) which avoid the conceptual problem we just described.

MDL and BIC

In the first paper on MDL, ?] used a two-part code and showed that, asymptotically, and under regularity conditions, the two-part codelength of xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} based on a k𝑘k-parameter model ℳℳ{\cal M} with an optimally discretized parameter space is given by

−log⁡P​(xn∣θ^​(xn))+k2​log⁡n,𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑛𝑘2𝑛-\log P(x^{n}\mid\hat{\theta}(x^{n}))+\frac{k}{2}\log n, (2.45)

thus ignoring O​(1)𝑂1O(1)-terms, which, as we have already seen, can be quite important. In the same year ?] showed that, for large enough n𝑛n, Bayesian model selection between two exponential families amounts to selecting the model minimizing (2.45), ignoring O​(1)𝑂1O(1)-terms as well. As a result of Schwarz’s paper, model selection based on (2.45) became known as the BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion). Not taking into account the functional form of the model ℳℳ{\cal M}, it often does not work very well in practice.

It has sometimes been claimed that MDL = BIC; for example, [2002, page 286] write “Rissanen’s result is equivalent to BIC”. This is wrong, even for the 1989 version of MDL that Burnham and Anderson refer to – as pointed out by ?], the BIC approximation only holds if the number of parameters k𝑘k is kept fixed and n𝑛n goes to infinity. If we select between nested families of models where the maximum number of parameters k𝑘k considered is either infinite or grows with n𝑛n, then model selection based on both P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} and on P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} tends to select quite different models than BIC - if k𝑘k gets closer to n𝑛n, the contribution to COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) of each additional parameter becomes much smaller than 0.5​log⁡n0.5𝑛0.5\log n [2004]. However, researchers who claim MDL = BIC have a good excuse: in early work, Rissanen himself has used the phrase ‘MDL criterion’ to refer to (2.45), and unfortunately, the phrase has stuck.

MDL and MML

MDL shares some ideas with the Minimum Message Length (MML) Principle which predates MDL by 10 years. Key references are [1968, 1975] and [1987]; a long list is in [2004]. Just as in MDL, MML chooses the hypothesis minimizing the code-length of the data. But the codes that are used are quite different from those in MDL. First of all, in MML one always uses two-part codes, so that MML automatically selects both a model family and parameter values. Second, while the MDL codes such as P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} minimize worst-case relative code-length (regret), the two-part codes used by MML are designed to minimize expected absolute code-length. Here the expectation is taken over a subjective prior distribution defined on the collection of models and parameters under consideration. While this approach contradicts Rissanen’s philosophy, in practice it often leads to similar results.

Indeed, Wallace and his co-workers stress that their approach is fully (subjective) Bayesian. Strictly speaking, a Bayesian should report his findings by citing the full posterior distribution. But sometimes one is interested in a single model, or hypothesis for the data. A good example is the inference of phylogenetic trees in biological applications: the full posterior would consist of a mixture of several of such trees, which might all be quite different from each other. Such a mixture is almost impossible to interpret – to get insight in the data we need a single tree. In that case, Bayesians often use the MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) hypothesis which maximizes the posterior, or the posterior mean parameter value. The first approach has some unpleasant properties, for example, it is not invariant under reparameterization. The posterior mean approach cannot be used if different model families are to be compared with each other. The MML method provides a theoretically sound way of proceeding in such cases.

2.9.3 MDL, Prequential Analysis and Cross-Validation

In a series of papers, A.P. Dawid [?, ?, ?] put forward a methodology for probability and statistics based on sequential prediction which he called the prequential approach. When applied to model selection problems, it is closely related to MDL - Dawid proposes to construct, for each model ℳ(j)superscriptℳ𝑗{\cal M}^{(j)} under consideration, a ‘probability forecasting system’ (a sequential prediction strategy) where the i+1𝑖1i+1-st outcome is predicted based on either the Bayesian posterior P¯Bayes​(θ|xi)subscript¯𝑃Bayesconditional𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}}(\theta|x^{i}) or on some estimator θ^​(xi)^𝜃superscript𝑥𝑖\hat{\theta}(x^{i}). Then the model is selected for which the associated sequential prediction strategy minimizes the accumulated prediction error. Related ideas were put forward by ?] under the name forward validation and ?]. From Section 2.6.4 we see that this is just a form of MDL - strictly speaking, every universal code can be thought of as as prediction strategy, but for the Bayesian and the plug-in universal models (Sections 2.6.3, 2.6.4) the interpretation is much more natural than for others20. Dawid mostly talks about such ‘predictive’ universal models. On the other hand, Dawid’s framework allows to adjust the prediction loss to be measured in terms of arbitrary loss functions, not just the log loss. In this sense, it is more general than MDL. Finally, the prequential idea goes beyond statistics: there is also a ‘prequential approach’ to probability theory developed by Dawid [1999] and ?].

Note that the prequential approach is similar in spirit to cross-validation. In this sense MDL is related to cross-validation as well. The main differences are that in MDL and the prequential approach, (1) all predictions are done sequentially (the future is never used to predict the past), and (2) each outcome is predicted exactly once.

2.9.4 Kolmogorov Complexity and Structure Function; Ideal MDL

Kolmogorov complexity [1997] has played a large but mostly inspirational role in Rissanen’s development of MDL. Over the last fifteen years, several ‘idealized’ versions of MDL have been proposed, which are more directly based on Kolmogorov complexity theory [1985, 1991, 1997, 2002]. These are all based on two-part codes, where hypotheses are described using a universal programming language such as C or Pascal. For example, in one proposal [1991], given data D𝐷D one picks the distribution minimizing

K​(P)+[−log⁡P​(D)],𝐾𝑃delimited-[]𝑃𝐷K(P)+\bigl{[}-\log P(D)\bigr{]}, (2.46)

where the minimum is taken over all computable probability distributions, and K​(P)𝐾𝑃K(P) is the length of the shortest computer program that, when input (x,d)𝑥𝑑(x,d), outputs P​(x)𝑃𝑥P(x) to d𝑑d bits precision. While such a procedure is mathematically well-defined, it cannot be used in practice. The reason is that in general, the P𝑃P minimizing (2.46) cannot be effectively computed. Kolmogorov himself used a variation of (2.46) in which one adopts, among all P𝑃P with K​(P)−log⁡P​(D)≈K​(D)𝐾𝑃𝑃𝐷𝐾𝐷K(P)-\log P(D)\approx K(D), the P𝑃P with smallest K​(P)𝐾𝑃K(P). Here K​(D)𝐾𝐷K(D) is the Kolmogorov complexity of D𝐷D, that is, the length of the shortest computer program that prints D𝐷D and then halts. This approach is known as the Kolmogorov structure function or minimum sufficient statistic approach [2004]. In this approach, the idea of separating data and noise (Section 2.6.1) is taken as basic, and the hypothesis selection procedure is defined in terms of it. The selected hypothesis may now be viewed as capturing all structure inherent in the data - given the hypothesis, the data cannot be distinguished from random noise. Therefore, it may be taken as a basis for lossy data compression – rather than sending the whole sequence, one only sends the hypothesis representing the ‘structure’ in the data. The receiver can then use this hypothesis to generate ‘typical’ data for it - this data should then ‘look just the same’ as the original data D. Rissanen views this separation idea as perhaps the most fundamental aspect of ‘learning by compression’. Therefore, in recent work he has tried to relate MDL (as defined here, based on lossless compression) to the Kolmogorov structure function, thereby connecting it to lossy compression, and, as he puts it, ‘opening up a new chapter in the MDL theory’ [2002, 2004, 2004].

Summary and Outlook

We have shown that MDL is closely related to, yet distinct from, several other methods for inductive inference. In the next section we discuss how well it performs compared to such other methods.

2.10 Problems for MDL?

Some authors have criticized MDL either on conceptual grounds (the idea makes no sense) [1996, 1999] or on practical grounds (sometimes it does not work very well in practice) [1997, 2003]. Are these criticisms justified? Let us consider them in turn.

2.10.1 Conceptual Problems: Occam’s Razor

The most-often heard conceptual criticisms are invariably related to Occam’s razor. We have already discussed in Section 1.5 of the previous chapter why we regard these criticisms as being entirely mistaken. Based on our newly acquired technical knowledge of MDL, let us discuss these criticisms a little bit further:

1. ‘Occam’s Razor (and MDL) is arbitrary’ (page 1.6)

If we restrict ourselves to refined MDL for comparing a finite number of models for which the NML distribution is well-defined, then there is nothing arbitrary about MDL - it is exactly clear what codes we should use for our inferences. The NML distribution and its close cousins, the Jeffreys’ prior marginal likelihood P¯Bayessubscript¯𝑃Bayes\bar{P}_{\text{\rm Bayes}} and the asymptotic expansion (2.24) are all invariant to continuous 1-to-1 reparameterizations of the model: parameterizing our model in a different way (choosing a different ‘description language’) does not change the inferred description lengths.

If we go beyond models for which the NML distribution is defined, and/or we compare an infinite set of models at the same time, then some ‘subjectivity’ is introduced – while there are still tough restrictions on the codes that we are allowed to use, all such codes prefer some hypotheses in the model over others. If one does not have an a priori preference over any of the hypotheses, one may interpret this as some arbitrariness being added to the procedure. But this ‘arbitrariness’ is of an infinitely milder sort than the arbitrariness that can be introduced if we allow completely arbitrary codes for the encoding of hypotheses as in crude two-part code MDL, Section 2.4.

  • Things get more subtle if we are interested not in model selection (find the best order Markov chain for the data) but in infinite-dimensional estimation (find the best Markov chain parameters for the data, among the set ℬℬ\mathcal{B} of all Markov chains of each order). In the latter case, if we are to apply MDL, we somehow have to carve up ℬℬ\mathcal{B} into subsets ℳ(0)⊆ℳ(1)⊆…⊆ℬsuperscriptℳ0superscriptℳ1…ℬ{\cal M}^{(0)}\subseteq{\cal M}^{(1)}\subseteq\ldots\subseteq\mathcal{B}. Suppose that we have already chosen ℳ(1)=ℬ(1)superscriptℳ1superscriptℬ1{\cal M}^{(1)}={\mathcal{B}}^{(1)} as the set of 111-st order Markov chains. We normally take ℳ(0)=ℬ(0)superscriptℳ0superscriptℬ0{\cal M}^{(0)}={\mathcal{B}}^{(0)} , the set of 00-th order Markov chains (Bernoulli distributions). But we could also have defined ℳ(0)superscriptℳ0{\cal M}^{(0)} as the set of all 111-st order Markov chains with P​(Xi+1=1∣Xi=1)=P​(Xi+1=0∣Xi=0)𝑃subscript𝑋𝑖1conditional1subscript𝑋𝑖1𝑃subscript𝑋𝑖1conditional0subscript𝑋𝑖0P(X_{i+1}=1\mid X_{i}=1)=P(X_{i+1}=0\mid X_{i}=0). This defines a one-dimensional subset of ℬ(1)superscriptℬ1{\mathcal{B}}^{(1)} that is not equal to ℬ(0)superscriptℬ0{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)}. While there are several good reasons21 for choosing ℬ(0)superscriptℬ0{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)} rather than ℳ(0)superscriptℳ0{\cal M}^{(0)}, there may be no indication that ℬ(0)superscriptℬ0{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)} is somehow a priori more likely than ℳ(0)superscriptℳ0{\cal M}^{(0)}. While MDL tells us that we somehow have to carve up the full set ℬℬ\mathcal{B}, it does not give us precise guidelines on how to do this – different carvings may be equally justified and lead to different inferences for small samples. In this sense, there is indeed some form of arbitrariness in this type of MDL applications. But this is unavoidable: we stress that this type of arbitrariness is enforced by all combined model/parameter selection methods - whether they be of the Structural Risk Minimization type [1998], AIC-type [2002], cross-validation or any other type. The only alternative is treating all hypotheses in the huge class ℬℬ\mathcal{B} on the same footing, which amounts to maximum likelihood estimation and extreme overfitting.

2. ‘Occam’s razor is false’ (page 1.6)

We often try to model real-world situations that can be arbitrarily complex, so why should we favor simple models? We gave in informal answer on page 1.6 where we claimed that even if the true data generating machinery is very complex, it may be a good strategy to prefer simple models for small sample sizes.

We are now in a position to give one formalization of this informal claim: it is simply the fact that MDL procedures, with their built-in preference for ‘simple’ models with small parametric complexity, are typically statistically consistent achieving good rates of convergence (page 1.5), whereas methods such as maximum likelihood which do not take model complexity into account are typically in-consistent whenever they are applied to complex enough models such as the set of polynomials of each degree or the set of Markov chains of all orders. This has implications for the quality of predictions: with complex enough models, no matter how many training data we observe, if we use the maximum likelihood distribution to predict future data from the same source, the prediction error we make will not converge to the prediction error that could be obtained if the true distribution were known; if we use an MDL submodel/parameter estimate (Section 2.8), the prediction error will converge to this optimal achieveable error.

Of course, consistency is not the only desirable property of a learning method, and it may be that in some particular settings, and under some particular performance measures, some alternatives to MDL outperform MDL. Indeed this can happen – see below. Yet it remains the case that all methods the author knows of that successfully deal with models of arbitrary complexity have a built-in preference for selecting simpler models at small sample sizes – methods such as Vapnik’s [?] Structural Risk Minimization, penalized minimum error estimators [1990] and the Akaike criterion [2002] all trade-off complexity with error on the data, the result invariably being that in this way, good convergence properties can be obtained. While these approaches measure ‘complexity’ in a manner different from MDL, and attach different relative weights to error on the data and complexity, the fundamental idea of finding a trade-off between ‘error’ and ‘complexity’ remains.

2.10.2 Practical Problems with MDL

We just described some perceived problems about MDL. Unfortunately, there are also some real ones: MDL is not a perfect method. While in many cases, the methods described here perform very well22 there are also cases where they perform suboptimally compared to other state-of-the-art methods. Often this is due to one of two reasons:

  1. 1.

    An asymptotic formula like (2.24) was used and the sample size was not large enough to justify this [2004].

  2. 2.

    P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} was undefined for the models under consideration, and this was solved by cutting off the parameter ranges at ad hoc values [2004].

In these cases the problem probably lies with the use of invalid approximations rather than with the MDL idea itself. More research is needed to find out when the asymptotics and other approximations can be trusted, and what is the ‘best’ way to deal with undefined P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}. For the time being, we suggest to avoid using (2.24) whenever possible, and to never cut off the parameter ranges at arbitrary values – instead, if COMPn​(ℳ)subscriptCOMP𝑛ℳ\text{\bf COMP}_{n}({\cal M}) becomes infinite, then some of the methods described in Section 2.7.2 should be used. Given these restrictions, P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}} and Bayesian inference with Jeffreys’ prior are the preferred methods, since they both achieve the minimax regret. If they are either ill-defined or computationally prohibitive for the models under consideration, one can use a prequential method or a sophisticated two-part code such as described by ?].

MDL and Misspecification

However, there is a class of problems where MDL is problematic in a more fundamental sense. Namely, if none of the distributions under consideration represents the data generating machinery very well, then both MDL and Bayesian inference may sometimes do a bad job in finding the ‘best’ approximation within this class of not-so-good hypotheses. This has been observed in practice23 [1997, 2002, 2003]. ?] show that MDL can behave quite unreasonably for some classification problems in which the true distribution is not in ℳℳ{\cal M}. This is closely related to the problematic behavior of MDL for classification tasks as mentioned in Section 2.8. All this is a bit ironic, since MDL was explicitly designed not to depend on the untenable assumption that some P∗∈ℳsuperscript𝑃ℳP^{*}\in{\cal M} generates the data. But empirically we find that while it generally works quite well if some P∗∈ℳsuperscript𝑃ℳP^{*}\in{\cal M} generates the data, it may sometimes fail if this is not the case.

2.11 Conclusion

MDL is a versatile method for inductive inference: it can be interpreted in at least four different ways, all of which indicate that it does something reasonable. It is typically asymptotically consistent, achieving good rates of convergence. It achieves all this without having been designed for consistency, being based on a philosophy which makes no metaphysical assumptions about the existence of ‘true’ distributions. All this strongly suggests that it is a good method to use in practice. Practical evidence shows that in many contexts it is, in other contexts its behavior can be problematic. In the author’s view, the main challenge for the future is to improve MDL for such cases, by somehow extending and further refining MDL procedures in a non ad-hoc manner. I am confident that this can be done, and that MDL will continue to play an important role in the development of statistical, and more generally, inductive inference.

Further Reading

MDL can be found on the web at www.mdl-research.org. Good places to start further exploration of MDL are [1998] and [2001]. Both papers provide excellent introductions, but they are geared towards a more specialized audience of information theorists and statisticians, respectively. Also worth reading is Rissanen’s [?] monograph. While outdated as an introduction to MDL methods, this famous ‘little green book’ still serves as a great introduction to Rissanen’s radical but appealing philosophy, which is described very eloquently.

Acknowledgments

The author would like to thank Jay Myung, Mark Pitt, Steven de Rooij and Teemu Roos, who read a preliminary version of this chapter and suggested several improvements.

Notes

1.1{1}.\ But see Section 2.9.4.

2.2{2}.\ Working directly with distributions on infinite sequences is more elegant, but it requires measure theory, which we want to avoid here.

3.3{3}.\ Also known as instantaneous codes and called, perhaps more justifiably, ‘prefix-free’ codes in [1997].

4.4{4}.\ For example, with non-integer codelengths the notion of ‘code’ becomes invariant to the size of the alphabet in which we describe data.

5.5{5}.\ As understood in elementary probability, i.e. with respect to Lebesgue measure.

6.6{6}.\ Even if one adopts a Bayesian stance and postulates that an agent can come up with a (subjective) distribution for every conceivable domain, this problem remains: in practice, the adopted distribution may be so complicated that we cannot design the optimal code corresponding to it, and have to use some ad hoc -instead.

7.7{7}.\ Henceforth, we simply use ‘model’ to denote probabilistic models; we typically use ℋℋ{\cal H} to denote sets of hypotheses such as polynomials, and reserve ℳℳ{\cal M} for probabilistic models.

8.8{8}.\ The terminology ‘crude MDL’ is not standard. It is introduced here for pedagogical reasons, to make clear the importance of having a single, unified principle for designing codes. It should be noted that Rissanen’s and Barron’s early theoretical papers on MDL already contain such principles, albeit in a slightly different form than in their recent papers. Early practical applications [1989, 1996] often do use ad hoc two-part codes which really are ‘crude’ in the sense defined here.

9.9{9}.\ See the previous endnote.

10.10{10}.\ but see [1998], Chapter 5 for more discussion.

11.11{11}.\ See Section 1.5 of Chapter 1 for a discussion on the role of consistency in MDL.

12.12{12}.\ See, for example [1991], [1985]

13.13{13}.\ Strictly speaking, the assumption that n𝑛n is given in advance (i.e., both encoder and decoder know n𝑛n) contradicts the earlier requirement that the code to be used for encoding hypotheses is not allowed to depend on n𝑛n. Thus, strictly speaking, we should first encode some n𝑛n explicitly, using 2​log⁡n+12𝑛12\log n+1 bits (Example 2.4), and then pick the n𝑛n (typically, but not necessarily equal to the actual sample size) that allows for the shortest three-part codelength of the data (first encode n𝑛n, then (k,θ)𝑘𝜃(k,\theta), then the data). In practice this will not significantly alter the chosen hypothesis, unless for some quite special data sequences.

14.14{14}.\ As explained in Figure 2.2, we identify these codes with their length functions, which is the only aspect we are interested in.

15.15{15}.\ The reason is that, in the full Bernoulli model with parameter θ∈[0,1]𝜃01\theta\in[0,1], the maximum likelihood estimator is given by n1/nsubscript𝑛1𝑛n_{1}/n, see Example 2.7. Since the likelihood log⁡P​(xn∣θ)𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃\log P(x^{n}\mid\theta) is a continuous function of θ𝜃\theta, this implies that if the frequency n1/nsubscript𝑛1𝑛n_{1}/n in xnsuperscript𝑥𝑛x^{n} is approximately (but not precisely) j/10𝑗10j/10, then the ML estimator in the restricted model {0.1,…,0.9}0.1…0.9\{0.1,\ldots,0.9\} is still given by θ^=j/10^𝜃𝑗10\hat{\theta}=j/10. Then log⁡P​(xn|θ)𝑃conditionalsuperscript𝑥𝑛𝜃\log P(x^{n}|\theta) is maximized by θ^=j/10^𝜃𝑗10\hat{\theta}=j/10, so that the L∈ℒ𝐿ℒL\in\mathcal{L} that minimizes codelength corresponds to θ=j/10𝜃𝑗10\theta=j/10.

16.16{16}.\ What we call ‘universal model’ in this text is known in the literature as a ‘universal model in the individual sequence sense’ – there also exist universal models in an ‘expected sense’, see Section 2.9.1. These lead to slightly different versions of MDL.

17.17{17}.\ To be fair, we should add that this naive version of GLRT is introduced here for educational purposes only. It is not recommended by any serious statistician!

18.18{18}.\ The standard definition of Fisher information [1997] is in terms of first derivatives of the log-likelihood; for most parametric models of interest, the present definition coincides with the standard one.

19.19{19}.\ The author has heard many people say this at many conferences. The reasons are probably historical: while the underlying philosophy has always been different, until Rissanen introduced the use of P¯nmlsubscript¯𝑃nml\bar{P}_{\text{\rm nml}}, most actual implementations of MDL ‘looked’ quite Bayesian.

20.20{20}.\ The reason is that the Bayesian and plug-in models can be interpreted as probabilistic sources. The NML and the two-part code models are no probabilistic sources, since P¯(n)superscript¯𝑃𝑛\bar{P}^{(n)} and P¯(n+1)\bar{P}^{(n+1}) are not compatible in the sense of Section 2.2.

21.21{21}.\ For example, ℬ(0)superscriptℬ0{\mathcal{B}}^{(0)} is better interpretable.

22.22{22}.\ We mention [2000, 2001] reporting excellent behavior of MDL in regression contexts; and [2003, 1999, 1998] reporting excellent behavior of predictive (prequential) coding in Bayesian network model selection and regression. Also, ‘objective Bayesian’ model selection methods are frequently and successfully used in practice [1996]. Since these are based on non-informative priors such as Jeffreys’, they often coincide with a version of refined MDL and thus indicate successful performance of MDL.

23.23{23}.\ But see ?] who point out that the problem of [1997] disappears if a more reasonable coding scheme is used.
